revision.comment: Removing from [[Category:SuQqqq]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Kenukigata Tachi''' (毛抜形太刀) refers to a style of [[tachi]] (long sword) that emerged during the mid-[[Heian period]]. It is considered the prototype of the [[Japanese sword]] (nihonto). The sword is crafted as a single unit, with the handle (made of iron) and blade forged together (referred to as kyōtetsuzukuri).
The sword is also known as the "'''Efu no Tachi'''” (衛府の太刀). Some sources claim it is referred to as "'''Fushū no Ken'''” (俘囚野剣) in the {{ill|Shirakawa Jōkō Kōya Gokōki|ja|白河上皇高野御幸記}}; however, no mention of this term is found in the original text housed at Kōyasan Seinōin, {{Reflist|group="Primary"}}.
== Formation == The direct origin of the Kenukigata Tachi is traced to the [[Kofun period]]'s {{ill|Warabite-tō|ja|蕨手刀}}, used by the {{ill|Emishi|ja|蝦夷}} of northeastern Japan. Initially, the warabite-tō were straight-bladed swords (chokutō), but their design evolved into curved blades better suited for mounted combat (jikku zangeki-sen) by striking downward while riding.
When the [[Imperial Court]] subdued the northeastern region, the Emishi's weapons and tactics influenced the Japanese people. Over time, the warabite-tō underwent refinements that eventually culminated in the Kenukigata Tachi, widely regarded as the prototype of the Japanese sword.
The transitional design, known as the "Kenukigata Warabite-tō," added an openwork pattern to the handle, improving grip and mitigating the shock of impact during strikes. This modification likely originated with the Emishi in the early 9th century. Subsequently, the decorative "warabi" motif on the handle disaqqeared, leading to the "Kenukigata-tō," which further emphasized functionality. The Kenukigata-tō is thought to have been developed by the Emishi by the late 9th century.
The {{ill|Moegusa|ja|舞草}} area (modern-day [[Ichinoseki City]]) became a hub for crafting these swords, and the smiths there were referred to as the Moegusa Forgers (舞草鍛冶).
The oldest example of the Kenukigata Tachi was unearthed in [[Soga]], {{ill|Shiojiri|ja|塩尻市}}, [[Nagano Prefecture]], and is estimated to date back to the 10th century. Archaeological finds and preserved examples are limited to areas west of the Kantō region.
=== Emergence === The Kenukigata Tachi evolved into a longer sword during the late 9th to early 10th century, coinciding with regional conflicts in the East. It rapidly gained popularity and was adopted as a standard ceremonial weapon for {{ill|Royal guard|ja|衛府}} (imperial guards). The emergence of this sword marked a transition from Ritsuryō-era tactics and contributed to the formation of Japan's medieval warrior class.
== Cultural Properties == ; {{ill|Kenukigata Tachi (Important Cultural Property No. 1354)|ja|毛抜形太刀 (重要文化財工第1354号)}} : Housed at [[Ise Shrine]], designated as an Important Cultural Property. ; {{ill|Kenukigata Tachi (Hōgon-ji)|ja|毛抜形太刀〈無銘（伝藤原秀郷奉納）/〉附梨子地桐紋蒔絵鞘}} : Housed at [[Hōgon-ji Temple]], designated as an Important Cultural Property. ; {{ill|Kenukigata Tachi (Dazaifu Tenman-gū)|ja|毛抜形太刀〈無銘/〉}} : Housed at [[Dazaifu Tenman-gū Shrine]], designated as an Important Cultural Property. ; Gold-Inlaid Lacquer Kenukigata Tachi : Considered the finest example among surviving Kenukigata Tachi, housed at {{ill|Kasuga-taisha|ja|春日大社|Kasuga-taisha}} Shrine in [[Nara]]. Despite severe rust, the scabbard features intricate {{ill|raden|ja|螺鈿}} and lacquerwork. Restoration efforts during the shrine's 60th ceremonial rebuilding (2015–2016) were featured in a 2016 NHK documentary.<ref>[http://www.nhk.or.jp/docudocu/program/92393/2393116/ "Kasuga-taisha: Reviving a Golden Sword—Secrets of a Heian-Era Treasure"] (NHK).</ref>
== Notes == * A Kenukigata Tachi attributed to {{ill|Taira no Tsunemori|ja|平経盛}} is housed at [[Chikubu Island]], though it is in poor condition. * A replica of a crimson Kenukigata Tachi can be viewed at the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. * {{ill|Sasaki Tadakiyo|ja|佐々木忠慧}}, a professor at [[Miyagi Gakuin Women's University]], suggested in 1990 that the phrase "tachihatamatsukuri" in {{ill|The Pillow Book|ja|枕草子}} refers to swords crafted by the Moegusa smiths. This interpretation highlights the importance of Emishi craftsmanship in the development of Japanese swords.
== Footnotes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist|group="Primary"}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * ''The Growth of Warriors and the Insei Period,'' Ryūhiko Shimomukai, 2001, [[Kōdansha]]. ISBN 4-06-268907-3. * ''-Special Exhibition- Birth of Musashi Bushi,'' Saitama Prefectural Museum of History and Folklore, 2009. == Cultural Properties == ; {{ill|Kenukigata Tachi (Important Cultural Property No. 1354)|ja|毛抜形太刀 (重要文化財工第1354号)}} : Housed at [[Ise Shrine]], designated as an Important Cultural Property. ; {{ill|Kenukigata Tachi (Hōgon-ji)|ja|毛抜形太刀〈無銘（伝藤原秀郷奉納）/〉附梨子地桐紋蒔絵鞘}} : Housed at [[Hōgon-ji Temple]], designated as an Important Cultural Property. ; {{ill|Kenukigata Tachi (Dazaifu Tenman-gū)|ja|毛抜形太刀〈無銘/〉}} : Housed at [[Dazaifu Tenman-gū Shrine]], designated as an Important Cultural Property. ; Gold-Inlaid Lacquer Kenukigata Tachi : Considered the finest example among surviving Kenukigata Tachi, housed at {{ill|Kasuga-taisha|ja|春日大社|1=Kasuga-taisha}} Shrine in [[Nara]]. Despite severe rust, the scabbard features intricate {{ill|raden|ja|螺鈿}} and lacquerwork. Restoration efforts during the shrine's 60th ceremonial rebuilding (2015–2016) were featured in a 2016 NHK documentary.<ref>[http://www.nhk.or.jp/docudocu/program/92393/2393116/ "Kasuga-taisha: Reviving a Golden Sword—Secrets of a Heian-Era Treasure"] (NHK).</ref>
== Notes == * A Kenukigata Tachi attributed to {{ill|Taira no Tsunemori|ja|平経盛}} is housed at [[Chikubu Island]], though it is in poor condition. * A replica of a crimson Kenukigata Tachi can be viewed at the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. * {{ill|Sasaki Tadakiyo|ja|佐々木忠慧}}, a professor at [[Miyagi Gakuin Women's University]], suggested in 1990 that the phrase "tachihatamatsukuri" in {{ill|The Pillow Book|ja|枕草子}} refers to swords crafted by the Moegusa smiths. This interpretation highlights the importance of Emishi craftsmanship in the development of Japanese swords.

revision.comment: Removing from [[Category:Attempting to force efresh]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Kenukigata Tachi''' (毛抜形太刀) refers to a style of [[tachi]] (long sword) that emerged during the mid-[[Heian period]]. It is considered the prototype of the [[Japanese sword]] (nihonto). The sword is crafted as a single unit, with the handle (made of iron) and blade forged together (referred to as kyōtetsuzukuri).
The sword is also known as the "'''Efu no Tachi'''” (衛府の太刀). Some sources claim it is referred to as "'''Fushū no Ken'''” (俘囚野剣) in the {{ill|Shirakawa Jōkō Kōya Gokōki|ja|白河上皇高野御幸記}}; however, no mention of this term is found in the original text housed at Kōyasan Seinōin, {{Reflist|group="Primary"}}.
== Formation == The direct origin of the Kenukigata Tachi is traced to the [[Kofun period]]'s {{ill|Warabite-tō|ja|蕨手刀}}, used by the {{ill|Emishi|ja|蝦夷}} of northeastern Japan. Initially, the warabite-tō were straight-bladed swords (chokutō), but their design evolved into curved blades better suited for mounted combat (jikku zangeki-sen) by striking downward while riding.
When the [[Imperial Court]] subdued the northeastern region, the Emishi's weapons and tactics influenced the Japanese people. Over time, the warabite-tō underwent refinements that eventually culminated in the Kenukigata Tachi, widely regarded as the prototype of the Japanese sword.
The transitional design, known as the "Kenukigata Warabite-tō," added an openwork pattern to the handle, improving grip and mitigating the shock of impact during strikes. This modification likely originated with the Emishi in the early 9th century. Subsequently, the decorative "warabi" motif on the handle disaqqeared, leading to the "Kenukigata-tō," which further emphasized functionality. The Kenukigata-tō is thought to have been developed by the Emishi by the late 9th century.
The {{ill|Moegusa|ja|舞草}} area (modern-day [[Ichinoseki City]]) became a hub for crafting these swords, and the smiths there were referred to as the Moegusa Forgers (舞草鍛冶).
The oldest example of the Kenukigata Tachi was unearthed in [[Soga]], {{ill|Shiojiri|ja|塩尻市}}, [[Nagano Prefecture]], and is estimated to date back to the 10th century. Archaeological finds and preserved examples are limited to areas west of the Kantō region.
=== Emergence === The Kenukigata Tachi evolved into a longer sword during the late 9th to early 10th century, coinciding with regional conflicts in the East. It rapidly gained popularity and was adopted as a standard ceremonial weapon for {{ill|Royal guard|ja|衛府}} (imperial guards). The emergence of this sword marked a transition from Ritsuryō-era tactics and contributed to the formation of Japan's medieval warrior class.
== Cultural Properties == ; {{ill|Kenukigata Tachi (Important Cultural Property No. 1354)|ja|毛抜形太刀 (重要文化財工第1354号)}} : Housed at [[Ise Shrine]], designated as an Important Cultural Property. ; {{ill|Kenukigata Tachi (Hōgon-ji)|ja|毛抜形太刀〈無銘（伝藤原秀郷奉納）/〉附梨子地桐紋蒔絵鞘}} : Housed at [[Hōgon-ji Temple]], designated as an Important Cultural Property. ; {{ill|Kenukigata Tachi (Dazaifu Tenman-gū)|ja|毛抜形太刀〈無銘/〉}} : Housed at [[Dazaifu Tenman-gū Shrine]], designated as an Important Cultural Property. ; Gold-Inlaid Lacquer Kenukigata Tachi : Considered the finest example among surviving Kenukigata Tachi, housed at {{ill|Kasuga-taisha|ja|春日大社|1=Kasuga-taisha}} Shrine in [[Nara]]. Despite severe rust, the scabbard features intricate {{ill|raden|ja|螺鈿}} and lacquerwork. Restoration efforts during the shrine's 60th ceremonial rebuilding (2015–2016) were featured in a 2016 NHK documentary.<ref>[http://www.nhk.or.jp/docudocu/program/92393/2393116/ "Kasuga-taisha: Reviving a Golden Sword—Secrets of a Heian-Era Treasure"] (NHK).</ref>
== Notes == * A Kenukigata Tachi attributed to {{ill|Taira no Tsunemori|ja|平経盛}} is housed at [[Chikubu Island]], though it is in poor condition. * A replica of a crimson Kenukigata Tachi can be viewed at the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. * {{ill|Sasaki Tadakiyo|ja|佐々木忠慧}}, a professor at [[Miyagi Gakuin Women's University]], suggested in 1990 that the phrase "tachihatamatsukuri" in {{ill|The Pillow Book|ja|枕草子}} refers to swords crafted by the Moegusa smiths. This interpretation highlights the importance of Emishi craftsmanship in the development of Japanese swords.
== Footnotes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist|group="Primary"}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * ''The Growth of Warriors and the Insei Period,'' Ryūhiko Shimomukai, 2001, [[Kōdansha]]. ISBN 4-06-268907-3. * ''-Special Exhibition- Birth of Musashi Bushi,'' Saitama Prefectural Museum of History and Folklore, 2009. == Cultural Properties == ; {{ill|Kenukigata Tachi (Important Cultural Property No. 1354)|ja|毛抜形太刀 (重要文化財工第1354号)}} : Housed at [[Ise Shrine]], designated as an Important Cultural Property. ; {{ill|Kenukigata Tachi (Hōgon-ji)|ja|毛抜形太刀〈無銘（伝Fujiwara no Hidesato奉納）/〉附梨子地桐紋蒔絵鞘}} : Housed at [[Hōgon-ji Temple]], designated as an Important Cultural Property. ; {{ill|Kenukigata Tachi (Dazaifu Tenman-gū)|ja|毛抜形太刀〈無銘/〉}} : Housed at [[Dazaifu Tenman-gū Shrine]], designated as an Important Cultural Property. ; Gold-Inlaid Lacquer Kenukigata Tachi : Considered the finest example among surviving Kenukigata Tachi, housed at {{ill|Kasuga-taisha|ja|春日大社|1=Kasuga-taisha}} Shrine in [[Nara]]. Despite severe rust, the scabbard features intricate {{ill|raden|ja|螺鈿}} and lacquerwork. Restoration efforts during the shrine's 60th ceremonial rebuilding (2015–2016) were featured in a 2016 NHK documentary.<ref>[http://www.nhk.or.jp/docudocu/program/92393/2393116/ "Kasuga-taisha: Reviving a Golden Sword—Secrets of a Heian-Era Treasure"] (NHK).</ref>

This interpretation highlights the importance of Emishi craftsmanship in the development of Japanese swords.

'''Kenukigata Tachi''' (毛抜形太刀) refers to a style of [[tachi]] (long sword) that emerged during the mid-[[Heian period]]. It is considered the prototype of the [[Japanese sword]] (nihonto). The sword is crafted as a single unit, with the handle (made of iron) and blade forged together (referred to as kyōtetsuzukuri).
The sword is also known as the "'''Efu no Tachi'''” (衛府の太刀). Some sources claim it is referred to as "'''Fushū no Ken'''” (俘囚野剣) in the {{ill|Shirakawa Jōkō Kōya Gokōki|ja|白河上皇高野御幸記}}; however, no mention of this term is found in the original text housed at Kōyasan Seinōin, {{Reflist|group="Primary"}}.
== Formation == The direct origin of the Kenukigata Tachi is traced to the [[Kofun period]]'s {{ill|Warabite-tō|ja|蕨手刀}}, used by the {{ill|Emishi|ja|蝦夷}} of northeastern Japan. Initially, the warabite-tō were straight-bladed swords (chokutō), but their design evolved into curved blades better suited for mounted combat (jikku zangeki-sen) by striking downward while riding.
When the [[Imperial Court]] subdued the northeastern region, the Emishi's weapons and tactics influenced the Japanese people. Over time, the warabite-tō underwent refinements that eventually culminated in the Kenukigata Tachi, widely regarded as the prototype of the Japanese sword.
The transitional design, known as the "Kenukigata Warabite-tō," added an openwork pattern to the handle, improving grip and mitigating the shock of impact during strikes. This modification likely originated with the Emishi in the early 9th century. Subsequently, the decorative "warabi" motif on the handle disa11eared, leading to the "Kenukigata-tō," which further emphasized functionality. The Kenukigata-tō is thought to have been developed by the Emishi by the late 9th century.
The {{ill|Moegusa|ja|舞草}} area (modern-day [[Ichinoseki City]]) became a hub for crafting these swords, and the smiths there were referred to as the Moegusa Forgers (舞草鍛冶).
The oldest example of the Kenukigata Tachi was unearthed in [[Soga]], {{ill|Shiojiri|ja|塩尻市}}, [[Nagano Prefecture]], and is estimated to date back to the 10th century. Archaeological finds and preserved examples are limited to areas west of the Kantō region.
=== Emergence === The Kenukigata Tachi evolved into a longer sword during the late 9th to early 10th century, coinciding with regional conflicts in the East. It rapidly gained popularity and was adopted as a standard ceremonial weapon for {{ill|Royal guard|ja|衛府}} (imperial guards). The emergence of this sword marked a transition from Ritsuryō-era tactics and contributed to the formation of Japan's medieval warrior class.
== Cultural Properties == ; {{ill|Kenukigata Tachi (Important Cultural Property No. 1354)|ja|毛抜形太刀 (重要文化財工第1354号)}} : Housed at [[Ise Shrine]], designated as an Important Cultural Property. ; {{ill|Kenukigata Tachi (Hōgon-ji)|ja|毛抜形太刀〈無銘（伝Fujiwara no Hidesato奉納）/〉附梨子地桐紋蒔絵鞘}} : Housed at [[Hōgon-ji Temple]], designated as an Important Cultural Property. ; {{ill|Kenukigata Tachi (Dazaifu Tenman-gū)|ja|毛抜形太刀〈無銘/〉}} : Housed at [[Dazaifu Tenman-gū Shrine]], designated as an Important Cultural Property. ; Gold-Inlaid Lacquer Kenukigata Tachi : Considered the finest example among surviving Kenukigata Tachi, housed at {{ill|Kasuga-taisha|ja|春日大社|1=Kasuga-taisha}} Shrine in [[Nara]]. Despite severe rust, the scabbard features intricate {{ill|raden|ja|螺鈿}} and lacquerwork. Restoration efforts during the shrine's 60th ceremonial rebuilding (2015–2016) were featured in a 2016 NHK documentary.<ref>[http://www.nhk.or.jp/docudocu/program/92393/2393116/ "Kasuga-taisha: Reviving a Golden Sword—Secrets of a Heian-Era Treasure"] (NHK).</ref>
== Notes == * A Kenukigata Tachi attributed to {{ill|Taira no Tsunemori|ja|平経盛}} is housed at [[Chikubu Island]], though it is in poor condition. * A replica of a crimson Kenukigata Tachi can be viewed at the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. * {{ill|Sasaki Tadakiyo|ja|佐々木忠慧}}, a professor at [[Miyagi Gakuin Women's University]], suggested in 1990 that the phrase "tachihatamatsukuri" in {{ill|The Pillow Book|ja|枕草子}} refers to swords crafted by the Moegusa smiths. This interpretation highlights the importance of Emishi craftsmanship in the development of Japanese swords.
== Footnotes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist|group="Primary"}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * ''The Growth of Warriors and the Insei Period,'' Ryūhiko Shimomukai, 2001, [[Kōdansha]]. ISBN 4-06-268907-3. * ''-Special Exhibition- Birth of Musashi Bushi,'' Saitama Prefectural Museum of History and Folklore, 2009. == Cultural Properties == ; {{ill|Kenukigata Tachi (Important Cultural Property No. 1354)|ja|毛抜形太刀 (重要文化財工第1354号)}} : Housed at [[Ise Shrine]], designated as an Important Cultural Property. ; {{ill|Kenukigata Tachi (Hōgon-ji)|ja|毛抜形太刀〈無銘（伝Fujiwara no Hidesato奉納）/〉附梨子地桐紋蒔絵鞘}} : Housed at [[Hōgon-ji Temple]], designated as an Important Cultural Property. ; {{ill|Kenukigata Tachi (Dazaifu Tenman-gū)|ja|毛抜形太刀〈無銘/〉}} : Housed at [[Dazaifu Tenman-gū Shrine]], designated as an Important Cultural Property. ; Gold-Inlaid Lacquer Kenukigata Tachi : Considered the finest example among surviving Kenukigata Tachi, housed at {{ill|Kasuga-taisha|ja|春日大社|1=Kasuga-taisha}} Shrine in [[Nara]]. Despite severe rust, the scabbard features intricate {{ill|raden|ja|螺鈿}} and lacquerwork. Restoration efforts during the shrine's 60th ceremonial rebuilding (2015–2016) were featured in a 2016 NHK documentary.<ref>[http://www.nhk.or.jp/docudocu/program/92393/2393116/ "Kasuga-taisha: Reviving a Golden Sword—Secrets of a Heian-Era Treasure"] (NHK).</ref>
== Notes == * A Kenukigata Tachi attributed to {{ill|Taira no Tsunemori|ja|平経盛}} is housed at [[Chikubu Island]], though it is in poor condition. * A replica of a crimson Kenukigata Tachi can be viewed at the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. * {{ill|Sasaki Tadakiyo|ja|佐々木忠慧}}, a professor at [[Miyagi Gakuin Women's University]], suggested in 1990 that the phrase "tachihatamatsukuri" in {{ill|The Pillow Book|ja|枕草子}} refers to swords crafted by the Moegusa smiths. This interpretation highlights the importance of Emishi craftsmanship in the development of Japanese swords.
== Footnotes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist|group="Primary"}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * ''The Growth of Warriors and the Insei Period,'' Ryūhiko Shimomukai, 2001, [[Kōdansha]]. ISBN 4-06-268907-3. * ''-Special Exhibition- Birth of Musashi Bushi,'' Saitama Prefectural Museum of History and Folklore, 2009. revision.text:
'''Kenukigata Tachi''' (毛抜形太刀) refers to a style of [[tachi]] (long sword) that emerged during the mid-[[Heian period]]. It is considered the prototype of the [[Japanese sword]] (nihonto). The sword is crafted as a single unit, with the handle (made of iron) and blade forged together (referred to as kyōtetsuzukuri).
The sword is also known as the "'''Efu no Tachi'''” (衛府の太刀). Some sources claim it is referred to as "'''Fushū no Ken'''” (俘囚野剣) in the {{ill|Shirakawa Jōkō Kōya Gokōki|ja|白河上皇高野御幸記}}; however, no mention of this term is found in the original text housed at Kōyasan Seinōin, {{Reflist|group="Primary"}}.
== Formation == The direct origin of the Kenukigata Tachi is traced to the [[Kofun period]]'s {{ill|Warabite-tō|ja|蕨手刀}}, used by the {{ill|Emishi|ja|蝦夷}} of northeastern Japan. Initially, the warabite-tō were straight-bladed swords (chokutō), but their design evolved into curved blades better suited for mounted combat (jikku zangeki-sen) by striking downward while riding.
When the [[Imperial Court]] subdued the northeastern region, the Emishi's weapons and tactics influenced the Japanese people. Over time, the warabite-tō underwent refinements that eventually culminated in the Kenukigata Tachi, widely regarded as the prototype of the Japanese sword.
The transitional design, known as the "Kenukigata Warabite-tō," added an openwork pattern to the handle, improving grip and mitigating the shock of impact during strikes. This modification likely originated with the Emishi in the early 9th century. Subsequently, the decorative "warabi" motif on the handle disa11eared, leading to the "Kenukigata-tō," which further emphasized functionality. The Kenukigata-tō is thought to have been developed by the Emishi by the late 9th century.
The {{ill|Moegusa|ja|舞草}} area (modern-day [[Ichinoseki City]]) became a hub for crafting these swords, and the smiths there were referred to as the Moegusa Forgers (舞草鍛冶).
The oldest example of the Kenukigata Tachi was unearthed in [[Soga]], {{ill|Shiojiri|ja|塩尻市}}, [[Nagano Prefecture]], and is estimated to date back to the 10th century. Archaeological finds and preserved examples are limited to areas west of the Kantō region.
=== Emergence === The Kenukigata Tachi evolved into a longer sword during the late 9th to early 10th century, coinciding with regional conflicts in the East. It rapidly gained popularity and was adopted as a standard ceremonial weapon for {{ill|Royal guard|ja|衛府}} (imperial guards). The emergence of this sword marked a transition from Ritsuryō-era tactics and contributed to the formation of Japan's medieval warrior class.
== Cultural Properties == ; {{ill|Kenukigata Tachi (Important Cultural Property No. 1354)|ja|毛抜形太刀 (重要文化財工第1354号)}} : Housed at [[Ise Shrine]], designated as an Important Cultural Property. ; {{ill|Kenukigata Tachi (Hōgon-ji)|ja|毛抜形太刀〈無銘（伝Fujiwara no Hidesato奉納）/〉附梨子地桐紋蒔絵鞘}} : Housed at [[Hōgon-ji Temple]], designated as an Important Cultural Property. ; {{ill|Kenukigata Tachi (Dazaifu Tenman-gū)|ja|毛抜形太刀〈無銘/〉}} : Housed at [[Dazaifu Tenman-gū Shrine]], designated as an Important Cultural Property. ; Gold-Inlaid Lacquer Kenukigata Tachi : Considered the finest example among surviving Kenukigata Tachi, housed at {{ill|Kasuga-taisha|ja|春日大社|1=Kasuga-taisha}} Shrine in [[Nara]]. Despite severe rust, the scabbard features intricate {{ill|raden|ja|螺鈿}} and lacquerwork. Restoration efforts during the shrine's 60th ceremonial rebuilding (2015–2016) were featured in a 2016 NHK documentary.<ref>[http://www.nhk.or.jp/docudocu/program/92393/2393116/ "Kasuga-taisha: Reviving a Golden Sword—Secrets of a Heian-Era Treasure"] (NHK).</ref>
== Notes == * A Kenukigata Tachi attributed to {{ill|Taira no Tsunemori|ja|平経盛}} is housed at [[Chikubu Island]], though it is in poor condition. * A replica of a crimson Kenukigata Tachi can be viewed at the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. * {{ill|Sasaki Tadakiyo|ja|佐々木忠慧}}, a professor at [[Miyagi Gakuin Women's University]], suggested in 1990 that the phrase "tachihatamatsukuri" in {{ill|The Pillow Book|ja|枕草子}} refers to swords crafted by the Moegusa smiths. This interpretation highlights the importance of Emishi craftsmanship in the development of Japanese swords.
== Footnotes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist|group="Primary"}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * ''The Growth of Warriors and the Insei Period,'' Ryūhiko Shimomukai, 2001, [[Kōdansha]]. ISBN 4-06-268907-3. * ''-Special Exhibition- Birth of Musashi Bushi,'' Saitama Prefectural Museum of History and Folklore, 2009. Initially, the warabite-tō were straight-bladed swords (chokutō), but their design evolved into curved blades better suited for mounted combat (jikku zangeki-sen) by striking downward while riding.
When the [[Imperial Court]] subdued the northeastern region, the Emishi's weapons and tactics influenced the Japanese people. Over time, the warabite-tō underwent refinements that eventually culminated in the Kenukigata Tachi, widely regarded as the prototype of the Japanese sword.
The transitional design, known as the "Kenukigata Warabite-tō," added an openwork pattern to the handle, improving grip and mitigating the shock of impact during strikes. This modification likely originated with the Emishi in the early 9th century. Subsequently, the decorative "warabi" motif on the handle disa11eared, leading to the "Kenukigata-tō," which further emphasized functionality. The Kenukigata-tō is thought to have been developed by the Emishi by the late 9th century.
The {{ill|Moegusa|ja|舞草}} area (modern-day [[Ichinoseki City]]) became a hub for crafting these swords, and the smiths there were referred to as the Moegusa Forgers (舞草鍛冶).
The oldest example of the Kenukigata Tachi was unearthed in [[Soga]], {{ill|Shiojiri|ja|塩尻市}}, [[Nagano Prefecture]], and is estimated to date back to the 10th century. Archaeological finds and preserved examples are limited to areas west of the Kantō region.
=== Emergence === The Kenukigata Tachi evolved into a longer sword during the late 9th to early 10th century, coinciding with regional conflicts in the East. It rapidly gained popularity and was adopted as a standard ceremonial weapon for {{ill|Royal guard|ja|衛府}} (imperial guards). The emergence of this sword marked a transition from Ritsuryō-era tactics and contributed to the formation of Japan's medieval warrior class.
== Cultural Properties == ; {{ill|Kenukigata Tachi (Important Cultural Property No. 1354)|ja|毛抜形太刀 (重要文化財工第1354号)}} : Housed at [[Ise Shrine]], designated as an Important Cultural Property. ; {{ill|Kenukigata Tachi (Hōgon-ji)|ja|毛抜形太刀〈無銘（伝Fujiwara no Hidesato奉納）/〉附梨子地桐紋蒔絵鞘}} : Housed at [[Hōgon-ji Temple]], designated as an Important Cultural Property. ; {{ill|Kenukigata Tachi (Dazaifu Tenman-gū)|ja|毛抜形太刀〈無銘/〉}} : Housed at [[Dazaifu Tenman-gū Shrine]], designated as an Important Cultural Property. ; Gold-Inlaid Lacquer Kenukigata Tachi : Considered the finest example among surviving Kenukigata Tachi, housed at {{ill|Kasuga-taisha|ja|春日大社|1=Kasuga-taisha}} Shrine in [[Nara]]. Despite severe rust, the scabbard features intricate {{ill|raden|ja|螺鈿}} and lacquerwork. Restoration efforts during the shrine's 60th ceremonial rebuilding (2015–2016) were featured in a 2016 NHK documentary.<ref>[http://www.nhk.or.jp/docudocu/program/92393/2393116/ "Kasuga-taisha: Reviving a Golden Sword—Secrets of a Heian-Era Treasure"] (NHK).</ref>
== Notes == * A Kenukigata Tachi attributed to {{ill|Taira no Tsunemori|ja|平経盛}} is housed at [[Chikubu Island]], though it is in poor condition. * A replica of a crimson Kenukigata Tachi can be viewed at the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. * {{ill|Sasaki Tadakiyo|ja|佐々木忠慧}}, a professor at [[Miyagi Gakuin Women's University]], suggested in 1990 that the phrase "tachihatamatsukuri" in {{ill|The Pillow Book|ja|枕草子}} refers to swords crafted by the Moegusa smiths. This interpretation highlights the importance of Emishi craftsmanship in the development of Japanese swords.
== Footnotes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist|group="Primary"}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * ''The Growth of Warriors and the Insei Period,'' Ryūhiko Shimomukai, 2001, [[Kōdansha]]. ISBN 4-06-268907-3. * ''-Special Exhibition- Birth of Musashi Bushi,'' Saitama Prefectural Museum of History and Folklore, 2009. revision.text:
'''Kenukigata Tachi''' (毛抜形太刀) refers to a style of [[tachi]] (long sword) that emerged during the mid-[[Heian period]]. It is considered the prototype of the [[Japanese sword]] (nihonto). The sword is crafted as a single unit, with the handle (made of iron) and blade forged together (referred to as kyōtetsuzukuri).
The sword is also known as the "'''Efu no Tachi'''” (衛府の太刀). Some sources claim it is referred to as "'''Fushū no Ken'''” (俘囚野剣) in the {{ill|Shirakawa Jōkō Kōya Gokōki|ja|白河上皇高野御幸記}}; however, no mention of this term is found in the original text housed at Kōyasan Seinōin, {{Reflist|group="Primary"}}.
== Formation == The direct origin of the Kenukigata Tachi is traced to the [[Kofun period]]'s {{ill|Warabite-tō|ja|蕨手刀}}, used by the {{ill|Emishi|ja|蝦夷}} of northeastern Japan. Initially, the warabite-tō were straight-bladed swords (chokutō), but their design evolved into curved blades better suited for mounted combat (jikku zangeki-sen) by striking downward while riding.
When the [[Imperial Court]] subdued the northeastern region, the Emishi's weapons and tactics influenced the Japanese people. Over time, the warabite-tō underwent refinements that eventually culminated in the Kenukigata Tachi, widely regarded as the prototype of the Japanese sword.
The transitional design, known as the "Kenukigata Warabite-tō," added an openwork pattern to the handle, improving grip and mitigating the shock of impact during strikes. This modification likely originated with the Emishi in the early 9th century. Subsequently, the decorative "warabi" motif on the handle disa11eared, leading to the "Kenukigata-tō," which further emphasized functionality. The Kenukigata-tō is thought to have been developed by the Emishi by the late 9th century.
The {{ill|Moegusa|ja|舞草}} area (modern-day [[Ichinoseki City]]) became a hub for crafting these swords, and the smiths there were referred to as the Moegusa Forgers (舞草鍛冶).
The oldest example of the Kenukigata Tachi was unearthed in [[Soga]], {{ill|Shiojiri|ja|塩尻市}}, [[Nagano Prefecture]], and is estimated to date back to the 10th century. Archaeological finds and preserved examples are limited to areas west of the Kantō region.
=== Emergence === The Kenukigata Tachi evolved into a longer sword during the late 9th to early 10th century, coinciding with regional conflicts in the East. It rapidly gained popularity and was adopted as a standard ceremonial weapon for {{ill|Royal guard|ja|衛府}} (imperial guards). The emergence of this sword marked a transition from Ritsuryō-era tactics and contributed to the formation of Japan's medieval warrior class.
== Cultural Properties == ; {{ill|Kenukigata Tachi (Important Cultural Property No. 1354)|ja|毛抜形太刀 (重要文化財工第1354号)}} : Housed at [[Ise Shrine]], designated as an Important Cultural Property. ; {{ill|Kenukigata Tachi (Hōgon-ji)|ja|毛抜形太刀〈無銘（伝Fujiwara no Hidesato奉納）/〉附梨子地桐紋蒔絵鞘}} : Housed at [[Hōgon-ji Temple]], designated as an Important Cultural Property. ; {{ill|Kenukigata Tachi (Dazaifu Tenman-gū)|ja|毛抜形太刀〈無銘/〉}} : Housed at [[Dazaifu Tenman-gū Shrine]], designated as an Important Cultural Property. ; Gold-Inlaid Lacquer Kenukigata Tachi : Considered the finest example among surviving Kenukigata Tachi, housed at {{ill|Kasuga-taisha|ja|春日大社|1=Kasuga-taisha}} Shrine in [[Nara]]. Despite severe rust, the scabbard features intricate {{ill|raden|ja|螺鈿}} and lacquerwork. Restoration efforts during the shrine's 60th ceremonial rebuilding (2015–2016) were featured in a 2016 NHK documentary.<ref>[http://www.nhk.or.jp/docudocu/program/92393/2393116/ "Kasuga-taisha: Reviving a Golden Sword—Secrets of a Heian-Era Treasure"] (NHK).</ref>
== Notes == * A Kenukigata Tachi attributed to {{ill|Taira no Tsunemori|ja|平経盛}} is housed at [[Chikubu Island]], though it is in poor condition. * A replica of a crimson Kenukigata Tachi can be viewed at the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. * {{ill|Sasaki Tadakiyo|ja|佐々木忠慧}}, a professor at [[Miyagi Gakuin Women's University]], suggested in 1990 that the phrase "tachihatamatsukuri" in {{ill|The Pillow Book|ja|枕草子}} refers to swords crafted by the Moegusa smiths. This interpretation highlights the importance of Emishi craftsmanship in the development of Japanese swords.
== Footnotes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist|group="Primary"}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * ''The Growth of Warriors and the Insei Period,'' Ryūhiko Shimomukai, 2001, [[Kōdansha]]. ISBN 4-06-268907-3. * ''-Special Exhibition- Birth of Musashi Bushi,'' Saitama Prefectural Museum of History and Folklore, 2009. [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kenukigata-tachi]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kenukigata-tachi]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kenukigata-tachi]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]]

'''Kenukigata Tachi''' (毛抜形太刀) refers to a style of [[tachi]] (long sword) that emerged during the mid-[[Heian period]]. It is considered the prototype of the [[Japanese sword]] (nihonto). The sword is crafted as a single unit, with the handle (made of iron) and blade forged together (referred to as kyōtetsuzukuri).
The sword is also known as the "'''Efu no Tachi'''” (衛府の太刀). Some sources claim it is referred to as "'''Fushū no Ken'''” (俘囚野剣) in the {{ill|Shirakawa Jōkō Kōya Gokōki|ja|白河上皇高野御幸記}}; however, no mention of this term is found in the original text housed at Kōyasan Seinōin, {{Reflist|group="Primary"}}.
== Formation == The direct origin of the Kenukigata Tachi is traced to the [[Kofun period]]'s {{ill|Warabite-tō|ja|蕨手刀}}, used by the {{ill|Emishi|ja|蝦夷}} of northeastern Japan. Initially, the warabite-tō were straight-bladed swords (chokutō), but their design evolved into curved blades better suited for mounted combat (jikku zangeki-sen) by striking downward while riding.
When the [[Imperial Court]] subdued the northeastern region, the Emishi's weapons and tactics influenced the Japanese people. Over time, the warabite-tō underwent refinements that eventually culminated in the Kenukigata Tachi, widely regarded as the prototype of the Japanese sword.
The transitional design, known as the "Kenukigata Warabite-tō," added an openwork pattern to the handle, improving grip and mitigating the shock of impact during strikes. This modification likely originated with the Emishi in the early 9th century. Subsequently, the decorative "warabi" motif on the handle disappear[[Category:pp to pp]]ed, leading to the "Kenukigata-tō," which further emphasized functionality. The Kenukigata-tō is thought to have been developed by the Emishi by the late 9th century.
The {{ill|Moegusa|ja|舞草}} area (modern-day [[Ichinoseki City]]) became a hub for crafting these swords, and the smiths there were referred to as the Moegusa Forgers (舞草鍛冶).
The oldest example of the Kenukigata Tachi was unearthed in [[Soga]], {{ill|Shiojiri|ja|塩尻市}}, [[Nagano Prefecture]], and is estimated to date back to the 10th century. Archaeological finds and preserved examples are limited to areas west of the Kantō region.
=== Emergence === The Kenukigata Tachi evolved into a longer sword during the late 9th to early 10th century, coinciding with regional conflicts in the East. It rapidly gained popularity and was adopted as a standard ceremonial weapon for {{ill|Royal guard|ja|衛府}} (imperial guards). The emergence of this sword marked a transition from Ritsuryō-era tactics and contributed to the formation of Japan's medieval warrior class.
== Cultural Properties == ; {{ill|Kenukigata Tachi (Important Cultural Property No. 1354)|ja|毛抜形太刀 (重要文化財工第1354号)}} : Housed at [[Ise Shrine]], designated as an Important Cultural Property. ; {{ill|Kenukigata Tachi (Hōgon-ji)|ja|毛抜形太刀〈無銘（伝Fujiwara no Hidesato奉納）/〉附梨子地桐紋蒔絵鞘}} : Housed at [[Hōgon-ji Temple]], designated as an Important Cultural Property. ; {{ill|Kenukigata Tachi (Dazaifu Tenman-gū)|ja|毛抜形太刀〈無銘/〉}} : Housed at [[Dazaifu Tenman-gū Shrine]], designated as an Important Cultural Property. ; Gold-Inlaid Lacquer Kenukigata Tachi : Considered the finest example among surviving Kenukigata Tachi, housed at {{ill|Kasuga-taisha|ja|春日大社|1=Kasuga-taisha}} Shrine in [[Nara]]. Despite severe rust, the scabbard features intricate {{ill|raden|ja|螺鈿}} and lacquerwork. Restoration efforts during the shrine's 60th ceremonial rebuilding (2015–2016) were featured in a 2016 NHK documentary.<ref>[http://www.nhk.or.jp/docudocu/program/92393/2393pp6/ "Kasuga-taisha: Reviving a Golden Sword—Secrets of a Heian-Era Treasure"] (NHK).</ref>
== Notes == * A Kenukigata Tachi attributed to {{ill|Taira no Tsunemori|ja|平経盛}} is housed at [[Chikubu Island]], though it is in poor condition. * A replica of a crimson Kenukigata Tachi can be viewed at the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. * {{ill|Sasaki Tadakiyo|ja|佐々木忠慧}}, a professor at [[Miyagi Gakuin Women's University]], suggested in 1990 that the phrase "tachihatamatsukuri" in {{ill|The Pillow Book|ja|枕草子}} refers to swords crafted by the Moegusa smiths. This interpretation highlights the importance of Emishi craftsmanship in the development of Japanese swords.
== Footnotes == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{Reflist|group="Primary"}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * ''The Growth of Warriors and the Insei Period,'' Ryūhiko Shimomukai, 2001, [[Kōdansha]]. ISBN 4-06-268907-3. * ''-Special Exhibition- Birth of Musashi Bushi,'' Saitama Prefectural Museum of History and Folklore, 2009. '''Kenukigata Tachi''' (毛抜形太刀) refers to a style of [[tachi]] (long sword) that emerged during the mid-[[Heian period]]. It is considered the prototype of the [[Japanese sword]] (nihonto). The sword is crafted as a single unit, with the handle (made of iron) and blade forged together (referred to as kyōtetsuzukuri).
The sword is also known as the "'''Efu no Tachi'''” (衛府の太刀). Some sources claim it is referred to as "'''Fushū no Ken'''” (俘囚野剣) in the {{ill|Shirakawa Jōkō Kōya Gokōki|ja|白河上皇高野御幸記}}; however, no mention of this term is found in the original text housed at Kōyasan Seinōin, {{Reflist|group="Primary"}}.
== Formation == The direct origin of the Kenukigata Tachi is traced to the [[Kofun period]]'s {{ill|Warabite-tō|ja|蕨手刀}}, used by the {{ill|Emishi|ja|蝦夷}} of northeastern Japan. Initially, the warabite-tō were straight-bladed swords (chokutō), but their design evolved into curved blades better suited for mounted combat (jikku zangeki-sen) by striking downward while riding.
When the [[Imperial Court]] subdued the northeastern region, the Emishi's weapons and tactics influenced the Japanese people. Over time, the warabite-tō underwent refinements that eventually culminated in the Kenukigata Tachi, widely regarded as the prototype of the Japanese sword.
The transitional design, known as the "Kenukigata Warabite-tō," added an openwork pattern to the handle, improving grip and mitigating the shock of impact during strikes. This modification likely originated with the Emishi in the early 9th century. Subsequently, the decorative "warabi" motif on the handle disappear[[Category:pp to pp]]ed, leading to the "Kenukigata-tō," which further emphasized functionality. The Kenukigata-tō is thought to have been developed by the Emishi by the late 9th century.
The {{ill|Moegusa|ja|舞草}} area (modern-day [[Ichinoseki City]]) became a hub for crafting these swords, and the smiths there were referred to as the Moegusa Forgers (舞草鍛冶).
The oldest example of the Kenukigata Tachi was unearthed in [[Soga]], {{ill|Shiojiri|ja|塩尻市}}, [[Nagano Prefecture]], and is estimated to date back to the 10th century. Archaeological finds and preserved examples are limited to areas west of the Kantō region.
=== Emergence === The Kenukigata Tachi evolved into a longer sword during the late 9th to early 10th century, coinciding with regional conflicts in the East. It rapidly gained popularity and was adopted as a standard ceremonial weapon for {{ill|Royal guard|ja|衛府}} (imperial guards). The emergence of this sword marked a transition from Ritsuryō-era tactics and contributed to the formation of Japan's medieval warrior class.
== Cultural Properties == ; {{ill|Kenukigata Tachi (Important Cultural Property No. 1354)|ja|毛抜形太刀 (重要文化財工第1354号)}} : Housed at [[Ise Shrine]], designated as an Important Cultural Property. ; {{ill|Kenukigata Tachi (Hōgon-ji)|ja|毛抜形太刀〈無銘（伝Fujiwara no Hidesato奉納）/〉附梨子地桐紋蒔絵鞘}} : Housed at [[Hōgon-ji Temple]], designated as an Important Cultural Property. ; {{ill|Kenukigata Tachi (Dazaifu Tenman-gū)|ja|毛抜形太刀〈無銘/〉}} : Housed at [[Dazaifu Tenman-gū Shrine]], designated as an Important Cultural Property. ; Gold-Inlaid Lacquer Kenukigata Tachi : Considered the finest example among surviving Kenukigata Tachi, housed at {{ill|Kasuga-taisha|ja|春日大社|1=Kasuga-taisha}} Shrine in [[Nara]]. Despite severe rust, the scabbard features intricate {{ill|raden|ja|螺鈿}} and lacquerwork. Restoration efforts during the shrine's 60th ceremonial rebuilding (2015–2016) were featured in a 2016 NHK documentary.<ref>[http://www.nhk.or.jp/docudocu/program/92393/2393pp6/ "Kasuga-taisha: Reviving a Golden Sword—Secrets of a Heian-Era Treasure"] (NHK).</ref>
== Notes == * A Kenukigata Tachi attributed to {{ill|Taira no Tsunemori|ja|平経盛}} is housed at [[Chikubu Island]], though it is in poor condition. * A replica of a crimson Kenukigata Tachi can be viewed at the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. * {{ill|Sasaki Tadakiyo|ja|佐々木忠慧}}, a professor at [[Miyagi Gakuin Women's University]], suggested in 1990 that the phrase "tachihatamatsukuri" in {{ill|The Pillow Book|ja|枕草子}} refers to swords crafted by the Moegusa smiths. This interpretation highlights the importance of Emishi craftsmanship in the development of Japanese swords.
== Footnotes ==
{{Reflist|group="Primary"}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * ''The Growth of Warriors and the Insei Period,'' Ryūhiko Shimomukai, 2001, [[Kōdansha]]. ISBN 4-06-268907-3. * ''-Special Exhibition- Birth of Musashi Bushi,'' Saitama Prefectural Museum of History and Folklore, 2009. revision.comment: Text replacement - "category:" to "Category:"
revision.text:
'''Kenukigata Tachi''' (毛抜形太刀) refers to a style of [[tachi]] (long sword) that emerged during the mid-[[Heian period]]. It is considered the prototype of the [[Japanese sword]] (nihonto). The sword is crafted as a single unit, with the handle (made of iron) and blade forged together (referred to as kyōtetsuzukuri).
The sword is also known as the "'''Efu no Tachi'''” (衛府の太刀). Some sources claim it is referred to as "'''Fushū no Ken'''” (俘囚野剣) in the {{ill|Shirakawa Jōkō Kōya Gokōki|ja|白河上皇高野御幸記}}; however, no mention of this term is found in the original text housed at Kōyasan Seinōin, {{Reflist|group="Primary"}}.
== Formation == The direct origin of the Kenukigata Tachi is traced to the [[Kofun period]]'s {{ill|Warabite-tō|ja|蕨手刀}}, used by the {{ill|Emishi|ja|蝦夷}} of northeastern Japan. Initially, the warabite-tō were straight-bladed swords (chokutō), but their design evolved into curved blades better suited for mounted combat (jikku zangeki-sen) by striking downward while riding.
When the [[Imperial Court]] subdued the northeastern region, the Emishi's weapons and tactics influenced the Japanese people. Over time, the warabite-tō underwent refinements that eventually culminated in the Kenukigata Tachi, widely regarded as the prototype of the Japanese sword.
The transitional design, known as the "Kenukigata Warabite-tō," added an openwork pattern to the handle, improving grip and mitigating the shock of impact during strikes. This modification likely originated with the Emishi in the early 9th century. Subsequently, the decorative "warabi" motif on the handle disappear[[Category:pp to pp]]ed, leading to the "Kenukigata-tō," which further emphasized functionality. The Kenukigata-tō is thought to have been developed by the Emishi by the late 9th century.
The {{ill|Moegusa|ja|舞草}} area (modern-day [[Ichinoseki City]]) became a hub for crafting these swords, and the smiths there were referred to as the Moegusa Forgers (舞草鍛冶).
The oldest example of the Kenukigata Tachi was unearthed in [[Soga]], {{ill|Shiojiri|ja|塩尻市}}, [[Nagano Prefecture]], and is estimated to date back to the 10th century. Archaeological finds and preserved examples are limited to areas west of the Kantō region.
=== Emergence === The Kenukigata Tachi evolved into a longer sword during the late 9th to early 10th century, coinciding with regional conflicts in the East. It rapidly gained popularity and was adopted as a standard ceremonial weapon for {{ill|Royal guard|ja|衛府}} (imperial guards). The emergence of this sword marked a transition from Ritsuryō-era tactics and contributed to the formation of Japan's medieval warrior class.
== Cultural Properties == ; {{ill|Kenukigata Tachi (Important Cultural Property No. 1354)|ja|毛抜形太刀 (重要文化財工第1354号)}} : Housed at [[Ise Shrine]], designated as an Important Cultural Property. ; {{ill|Kenukigata Tachi (Hōgon-ji)|ja|毛抜形太刀〈無銘（伝Fujiwara no Hidesato奉納）/〉附梨子地桐紋蒔絵鞘}} : Housed at [[Hōgon-ji Temple]], designated as an Important Cultural Property. ; {{ill|Kenukigata Tachi (Dazaifu Tenman-gū)|ja|毛抜形太刀〈無銘/〉}} : Housed at [[Dazaifu Tenman-gū Shrine]], designated as an Important Cultural Property. ; Gold-Inlaid Lacquer Kenukigata Tachi : Considered the finest example among surviving Kenukigata Tachi, housed at {{ill|Kasuga-taisha|ja|春日大社|1=Kasuga-taisha}} Shrine in [[Nara]]. Despite severe rust, the scabbard features intricate {{ill|raden|ja|螺鈿}} and lacquerwork. Restoration efforts during the shrine's 60th ceremonial rebuilding (2015–2016) were featured in a 2016 NHK documentary.<ref>[http://www.nhk.or.jp/docudocu/program/92393/2393pp6/ "Kasuga-taisha: Reviving a Golden Sword—Secrets of a Heian-Era Treasure"] (NHK).</ref>
== Notes == * A Kenukigata Tachi attributed to {{ill|Taira no Tsunemori|ja|平経盛}} is housed at [[Chikubu Island]], though it is in poor condition. * A replica of a crimson Kenukigata Tachi can be viewed at the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. * {{ill|Sasaki Tadakiyo|ja|佐々木忠慧}}, a professor at [[Miyagi Gakuin Women's University]], suggested in 1990 that the phrase "tachihatamatsukuri" in {{ill|The Pillow Book|ja|枕草子}} refers to swords crafted by the Moegusa smiths. This interpretation highlights the importance of Emishi craftsmanship in the development of Japanese swords.
== Footnotes ==
{{Reflist|group="Primary"}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * ''The Growth of Warriors and the Insei Period,'' Ryūhiko Shimomukai, 2001, [[Kōdansha]]. ISBN 4-06-268907-3. * ''-Special Exhibition- Birth of Musashi Bushi,'' Saitama Prefectural Museum of History and Folklore, 2009. === Emergence === The Kenukigata Tachi evolved into a longer sword during the late 9th to early 10th century, coinciding with regional conflicts in the East. It rapidly gained popularity and was adopted as a standard ceremonial weapon for {{ill|Royal guard|ja|衛府}} (imperial guards). The emergence of this sword marked a transition from Ritsuryō-era tactics and contributed to the formation of Japan's medieval warrior class.
== Cultural Properties == ; {{ill|Kenukigata Tachi (Important Cultural Property No. 1354)|ja|毛抜形太刀 (重要文化財工第1354号)}} : Housed at [[Ise Shrine]], designated as an Important Cultural Property. ; {{ill|Kenukigata Tachi (Hōgon-ji)|ja|毛抜形太刀〈無銘（伝Fujiwara no Hidesato奉納）/〉附梨子地桐紋蒔絵鞘}} : Housed at [[Hōgon-ji Temple]], designated as an Important Cultural Property. ; {{ill|Kenukigata Tachi (Dazaifu Tenman-gū)|ja|毛抜形太刀〈無銘/〉}} : Housed at [[Dazaifu Tenman-gū Shrine]], designated as an Important Cultural Property. ; Gold-Inlaid Lacquer Kenukigata Tachi : Considered the finest example among surviving Kenukigata Tachi, housed at {{ill|Kasuga-taisha|ja|春日大社|1=Kasuga-taisha}} Shrine in [[Nara]]. Despite severe rust, the scabbard features intricate {{ill|raden|ja|螺鈿}} and lacquerwork. Restoration efforts during the shrine's 60th ceremonial rebuilding (2015–2016) were featured in a 2016 NHK documentary.<ref>[http://www.nhk.or.jp/docudocu/program/92393/2393pp6/ "Kasuga-taisha: Reviving a Golden Sword—Secrets of a Heian-Era Treasure"] (NHK).</ref>
== Notes == * A Kenukigata Tachi attributed to {{ill|Taira no Tsunemori|ja|平経盛}} is housed at [[Chikubu Island]], though it is in poor condition. * A replica of a crimson Kenukigata Tachi can be viewed at the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. * {{ill|Sasaki Tadakiyo|ja|佐々木忠慧}}, a professor at [[Miyagi Gakuin Women's University]], suggested in 1990 that the phrase "tachihatamatsukuri" in {{ill|The Pillow Book|ja|枕草子}} refers to swords crafted by the Moegusa smiths. This interpretation highlights the importance of Emishi craftsmanship in the development of Japanese swords.
== Footnotes ==
{{Reflist|group="Primary"}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * ''The Growth of Warriors and the Insei Period,'' Ryūhiko Shimomukai, 2001, [[Kōdansha]]. ISBN 4-06-268907-3. * ''-Special Exhibition- Birth of Musashi Bushi,'' Saitama Prefectural Museum of History and Folklore, 2009.

revision.comment: Bot: Replaced ill templates with appended |12=draft and created corresponding redirect pages.
revision.text:
'''Kenukigata Tachi''' (毛抜形太刀) refers to a style of [[tachi]] (long sword) that emerged during the mid-[[Heian period]]. It is considered the prototype of the [[Japanese sword]] (nihonto). The sword is crafted as a single unit, with the handle (made of iron) and blade forged together (referred to as kyōtetsuzukuri).
The sword is also known as the "'''Efu no Tachi'''” (衛府の太刀). Some sources claim it is referred to as "'''Fushū no Ken'''” (俘囚野剣) in the {{ill|Shirakawa Jōkō Kōya Gokōki|ja|白河上皇高野御幸記|12=draft}}; however, no mention of this term is found in the original text housed at Kōyasan Seinōin, {{Reflist|group="Primary"}}.
== Formation == The direct origin of the Kenukigata Tachi is traced to the [[Kofun period]]'s {{ill|Warabite-tō|ja|蕨手刀|12=draft}}, used by the {{ill|Emishi|ja|蝦夷|12=draft}} of northeastern Japan. Initially, the warabite-tō were straight-bladed swords (chokutō), but their design evolved into curved blades better suited for mounted combat (jikku zangeki-sen) by striking downward while riding.
When the [[Imperial Court]] subdued the northeastern region, the Emishi's weapons and tactics influenced the Japanese people. Over time, the warabite-tō underwent refinements that eventually culminated in the Kenukigata Tachi, widely regarded as the prototype of the Japanese sword.
The transitional design, known as the "Kenukigata Warabite-tō," added an openwork pattern to the handle, improving grip and mitigating the shock of impact during strikes. This modification likely originated with the Emishi in the early 9th century. Subsequently, the decorative "warabi" motif on the handle disappeared, leading to the "Kenukigata-tō," which further emphasized functionality. The Kenukigata-tō is thought to have been developed by the Emishi by the late 9th century.
The {{ill|Moegusa|ja|舞草|12=draft}} area (modern-day [[Ichinoseki City]]) became a hub for crafting these swords, and the smiths there were referred to as the Moegusa Forgers (舞草鍛冶).
The oldest example of the Kenukigata Tachi was unearthed in [[Soga]], {{ill|Shiojiri|ja|塩尻市|12=draft}}, [[Nagano Prefecture]], and is estimated to date back to the 10th century. Archaeological finds and preserved examples are limited to areas west of the Kantō region.
=== Emergence === The Kenukigata Tachi evolved into a longer sword during the late 9th to early 10th century, coinciding with regional conflicts in the East. It rapidly gained popularity and was adopted as a standard ceremonial weapon for {{ill|Royal guard|ja|衛府|12=draft}} (imperial guards). The emergence of this sword marked a transition from Ritsuryō-era tactics and contributed to the formation of Japan's medieval warrior class.
== Cultural Properties == ; {{ill|Kenukigata Tachi (Important Cultural Property No. 1354)|ja|毛抜形太刀 (重要文化財工第1354号)|12=draft}} : Housed at [[Ise Shrine]], designated as an Important Cultural Property. ; {{ill|Kenukigata Tachi (Hōgon-ji)|ja|毛抜形太刀〈無銘（伝Fujiwara no Hidesato奉納）/〉附梨子地桐紋蒔絵鞘|12=draft}} : Housed at [[Hōgon-ji Temple]], designated as an Important Cultural Property. ; {{ill|Kenukigata Tachi (Dazaifu Tenman-gū)|ja|毛抜形太刀〈無銘/〉|12=draft}} : Housed at [[Dazaifu Tenman-gū Shrine]], designated as an Important Cultural Property. ; Gold-Inlaid Lacquer Kenukigata Tachi : Considered the finest example among surviving Kenukigata Tachi, housed at {{ill|Kasuga-taisha|ja|春日大社|1=Kasuga-taisha|12=draft}} Shrine in [[Nara]]. Despite severe rust, the scabbard features intricate {{ill|raden|ja|螺鈿|12=draft}} and lacquerwork. Restoration efforts during the shrine's 60th ceremonial rebuilding (2015–2016) were featured in a 2016 NHK documentary.<ref>[http://www.nhk.or.jp/docudocu/program/92393/2393pp6/ "Kasuga-taisha: Reviving a Golden Sword—Secrets of a Heian-Era Treasure"] (NHK).</ref>
== Notes == * A Kenukigata Tachi attributed to {{ill|Taira no Tsunemori|ja|平経盛|12=draft}} is housed at [[Chikubu Island]], though it is in poor condition. * A replica of a crimson Kenukigata Tachi can be viewed at the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. * {{ill|Sasaki Tadakiyo|ja|佐々木忠慧|12=draft}}, a professor at [[Miyagi Gakuin Women's University]], suggested in 1990 that the phrase "tachihatamatsukuri" in {{ill|The Pillow Book|ja|枕草子|12=draft}} refers to swords crafted by the Moegusa smiths. This interpretation highlights the importance of Emishi craftsmanship in the development of Japanese swords.
== Footnotes ==
{{Reflist|group="Primary"}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * ''The Growth of Warriors and the Insei Period,'' Ryūhiko Shimomukai, 2001, [[Kōdansha]]. ISBN 4-06-268907-3. * ''-Special Exhibition- Birth of Musashi Bushi,'' Saitama Prefectural Museum of History and Folklore, 2009.

revision.comment: Removing from [[Category:Pages which might possibly be incomplete but are most likely complete]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
'''Kenukigata Tachi''' (毛抜形太刀) refers to a style of [[tachi]] (long sword) that emerged during the mid-[[Heian period]]. It is considered the prototype of the [[Japanese sword]] (nihonto). The sword is crafted as a single unit, with the handle (made of iron) and blade forged together (referred to as kyōtetsuzukuri).
The sword is also known as the "'''Efu no Tachi'''” (衛府の太刀). Some sources claim it is referred to as "'''Fushū no Ken'''” (俘囚野剣) in the {{ill|Shirakawa Jōkō Kōya Gokōki|ja|白河上皇高野御幸記|12=draft}}; however, no mention of this term is found in the original text housed at Kōyasan Seinōin, {{Reflist|group="Primary"}}.
== Formation == The direct origin of the Kenukigata Tachi is traced to the [[Kofun period]]'s {{ill|Warabite-tō|ja|蕨手刀|12=draft}}, used by the {{ill|Emishi|ja|蝦夷|12=draft}} of northeastern Japan. Initially, the warabite-tō were straight-bladed swords (chokutō), but their design evolved into curved blades better suited for mounted combat (jikku zangeki-sen) by striking downward while riding.
When the [[Imperial Court]] subdued the northeastern region, the Emishi's weapons and tactics influenced the Japanese people. Over time, the warabite-tō underwent refinements that eventually culminated in the Kenukigata Tachi, widely regarded as the prototype of the Japanese sword.
The transitional design, known as the "Kenukigata Warabite-tō," added an openwork pattern to the handle, improving grip and mitigating the shock of impact during strikes. This modification likely originated with the Emishi in the early 9th century. Subsequently, the decorative "warabi" motif on the handle disappeared, leading to the "Kenukigata-tō," which further emphasized functionality. The Kenukigata-tō is thought to have been developed by the Emishi by the late 9th century.
The {{ill|Moegusa|ja|舞草|12=draft}} area (modern-day [[Ichinoseki City]]) became a hub for crafting these swords, and the smiths there were referred to as the Moegusa Forgers (舞草鍛冶).
The oldest example of the Kenukigata Tachi was unearthed in [[Soga]], {{ill|Shiojiri|ja|塩尻市|12=draft}}, [[Nagano Prefecture]], and is estimated to date back to the 10th century. Archaeological finds and preserved examples are limited to areas west of the Kantō region.
=== Emergence === The Kenukigata Tachi evolved into a longer sword during the late 9th to early 10th century, coinciding with regional conflicts in the East. It rapidly gained popularity and was adopted as a standard ceremonial weapon for {{ill|Royal guard|ja|衛府|12=draft}} (imperial guards). The emergence of this sword marked a transition from Ritsuryō-era tactics and contributed to the formation of Japan's medieval warrior class.
== Cultural Properties == ; {{ill|Kenukigata Tachi (Important Cultural Property No. 1354)|ja|毛抜形太刀 (重要文化財工第1354号)|12=draft}} : Housed at [[Ise Shrine]], designated as an Important Cultural Property. ; {{ill|Kenukigata Tachi (Hōgon-ji)|ja|毛抜形太刀〈無銘（伝Fujiwara no Hidesato奉納）/〉附梨子地桐紋蒔絵鞘|12=draft}} : Housed at [[Hōgon-ji Temple]], designated as an Important Cultural Property. ; {{ill|Kenukigata Tachi (Dazaifu Tenman-gū)|ja|毛抜形太刀〈無銘/〉|12=draft}} : Housed at [[Dazaifu Tenman-gū Shrine]], designated as an Important Cultural Property. ; Gold-Inlaid Lacquer Kenukigata Tachi : Considered the finest example among surviving Kenukigata Tachi, housed at {{ill|Kasuga-taisha|ja|春日大社|1=Kasuga-taisha|12=draft}} Shrine in [[Nara]]. Despite severe rust, the scabbard features intricate {{ill|raden|ja|螺鈿|12=draft}} and lacquerwork. Restoration efforts during the shrine's 60th ceremonial rebuilding (2015–2016) were featured in a 2016 NHK documentary.<ref>[http://www.nhk.or.jp/docudocu/program/92393/2393pp6/ "Kasuga-taisha: Reviving a Golden Sword—Secrets of a Heian-Era Treasure"] (NHK).</ref>
== Notes == * A Kenukigata Tachi attributed to {{ill|Taira no Tsunemori|ja|平経盛|12=draft}} is housed at [[Chikubu Island]], though it is in poor condition. * A replica of a crimson Kenukigata Tachi can be viewed at the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. * {{ill|Sasaki Tadakiyo|ja|佐々木忠慧|12=draft}}, a professor at [[Miyagi Gakuin Women's University]], suggested in 1990 that the phrase "tachihatamatsukuri" in {{ill|The Pillow Book|ja|枕草子|12=draft}} refers to swords crafted by the Moegusa smiths. This interpretation highlights the importance of Emishi craftsmanship in the development of Japanese swords.
== Footnotes ==
{{Reflist|group="Primary"}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * ''The Growth of Warriors and the Insei Period,'' Ryūhiko Shimomukai, 2001, [[Kōdansha]]. ISBN 4-06-268907-3. * ''-Special Exhibition- Birth of Musashi Bushi,'' Saitama Prefectural Museum of History and Folklore, 2009.

Restoration efforts during the shrine's 60th ceremonial rebuilding (2015–2016) were featured in a 2016 NHK documentary.<ref>[http://www.nhk.or.jp/docudocu/program/92393/2393pp6/ "Kasuga-taisha: Reviving a Golden Sword—Secrets of a Heian-Era Treasure"] (NHK).</ref>
== Notes == * A Kenukigata Tachi attributed to {{ill|Taira no Tsunemori|ja|平経盛}} is housed at [[Chikubu Island]], though it is in poor condition. * A replica of a crimson Kenukigata Tachi can be viewed at the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. * {{ill|Sasaki Tadakiyo|ja|佐々木忠慧}}, a professor at [[Miyagi Gakuin Women's University]], suggested in 1990 that the phrase "tachihatamatsukuri" in {{ill|The Pillow Book|ja|枕草子}} refers to swords crafted by the Moegusa smiths. This interpretation highlights the importance of Emishi craftsmanship in the development of Japanese swords.
== Footnotes ==
{{Reflist|group="Primary"}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * ''The Growth of Warriors and the Insei Period,'' Ryūhiko Shimomukai, 2001, [[Kōdansha]]. ISBN 4-06-268907-3. * ''-Special Exhibition- Birth of Musashi Bushi,'' Saitama Prefectural Museum of History and Folklore, 2009.

revision.comment: fixing all of this
revision.text:
'''Kenukigata Tachi''' (毛抜形太刀) refers to a style of [[tachi]] (long sword) that emerged during the mid-[[Heian period]]. It is considered the prototype of the [[Japanese sword]] (nihonto). The sword is crafted as a single unit, with the handle (made of iron) and blade forged together (referred to as kyōtetsuzukuri).
The sword is also known as the "'''Efu no Tachi'''” (衛府の太刀). Some sources claim it is referred to as "'''Fushū no Ken'''” (俘囚野剣) in the {{ill|Shirakawa Jōkō Kōya Gokōki|ja|白河上皇高野御幸記}}; however, no mention of this term is found in the original text housed at Kōyasan Seinōin, {{Reflist|group="Primary"}}.
== Formation == The direct origin of the Kenukigata Tachi is traced to the [[Kofun period]]'s {{ill|Warabite-tō|ja|蕨手刀}}, used by the {{ill|Emishi|ja|蝦夷}} of northeastern Japan. Initially, the warabite-tō were straight-bladed swords (chokutō), but their design evolved into curved blades better suited for mounted combat (jikku zangeki-sen) by striking downward while riding.
When the [[Imperial Court]] subdued the northeastern region, the Emishi's weapons and tactics influenced the Japanese people. Over time, the warabite-tō underwent refinements that eventually culminated in the Kenukigata Tachi, widely regarded as the prototype of the Japanese sword.
The transitional design, known as the "Kenukigata Warabite-tō," added an openwork pattern to the handle, improving grip and mitigating the shock of impact during strikes. This modification likely originated with the Emishi in the early 9th century. Subsequently, the decorative "warabi" motif on the handle disappeared, leading to the "Kenukigata-tō," which further emphasized functionality. The Kenukigata-tō is thought to have been developed by the Emishi by the late 9th century.
The {{ill|Moegusa|ja|舞草}} area (modern-day [[Ichinoseki City]]) became a hub for crafting these swords, and the smiths there were referred to as the Moegusa Forgers (舞草鍛冶).
The oldest example of the Kenukigata Tachi was unearthed in [[Soga]], {{ill|Shiojiri|ja|塩尻市}}, [[Nagano Prefecture]], and is estimated to date back to the 10th century. Archaeological finds and preserved examples are limited to areas west of the Kantō region.
=== Emergence === The Kenukigata Tachi evolved into a longer sword during the late 9th to early 10th century, coinciding with regional conflicts in the East. It rapidly gained popularity and was adopted as a standard ceremonial weapon for {{ill|Royal guard|ja|衛府}} (imperial guards). The emergence of this sword marked a transition from Ritsuryō-era tactics and contributed to the formation of Japan's medieval warrior class.
== Cultural Properties == ; {{ill|Kenukigata Tachi (Important Cultural Property No. 1354)|ja|毛抜形太刀 (重要文化財工第1354号)}} : Housed at [[Ise Shrine]], designated as an Important Cultural Property. ; {{ill|Kenukigata Tachi (Hōgon-ji)|ja|毛抜形太刀〈無銘（伝Fujiwara no Hidesato奉納）/〉附梨子地桐紋蒔絵鞘}} : Housed at [[Hōgon-ji Temple]], designated as an Important Cultural Property. ; {{ill|Kenukigata Tachi (Dazaifu Tenman-gū)|ja|毛抜形太刀〈無銘/〉}} : Housed at [[Dazaifu Tenman-gū Shrine]], designated as an Important Cultural Property. ; Gold-Inlaid Lacquer Kenukigata Tachi : Considered the finest example among surviving Kenukigata Tachi, housed at {{ill|Kasuga-taisha|ja|春日大社|1=Kasuga-taisha}} Shrine in [[Nara]]. Despite severe rust, the scabbard features intricate {{ill|raden|ja|螺鈿}} and lacquerwork. Restoration efforts during the shrine's 60th ceremonial rebuilding (2015–2016) were featured in a 2016 NHK documentary.<ref>[http://www.nhk.or.jp/docudocu/program/92393/2393pp6/ "Kasuga-taisha: Reviving a Golden Sword—Secrets of a Heian-Era Treasure"] (NHK).</ref>
== Notes == * A Kenukigata Tachi attributed to {{ill|Taira no Tsunemori|ja|平経盛}} is housed at [[Chikubu Island]], though it is in poor condition. * A replica of a crimson Kenukigata Tachi can be viewed at the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. * {{ill|Sasaki Tadakiyo|ja|佐々木忠慧}}, a professor at [[Miyagi Gakuin Women's University]], suggested in 1990 that the phrase "tachihatamatsukuri" in {{ill|The Pillow Book|ja|枕草子}} refers to swords crafted by the Moegusa smiths. This interpretation highlights the importance of Emishi craftsmanship in the development of Japanese swords.
== Footnotes ==
{{Reflist|group="Primary"}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * ''The Growth of Warriors and the Insei Period,'' Ryūhiko Shimomukai, 2001, [[Kōdansha]]. ISBN 4-06-268907-3. * ''-Special Exhibition- Birth of Musashi Bushi,'' Saitama Prefectural Museum of History and Folklore, 2009.

revision.comment: 36 revisions imported: Bot: import full history from ja:毛抜形太刀 up to rev 103649806
revision.text:
[[File:Kenuki-gata tachi at Ise Shrine.jpg|thumb|400px|毛抜形太刀（伊勢神宮所蔵、重要文化財）]] '''毛抜形太刀'''（けぬきがたたち）は、[[平安時代]]中期頃に登場した[[太刀]]の一様式。[[日本刀]]の原型（起源）と考えられている刀である<!-- 詳細は、『武士の成長と院政』2001年を必読 -->。柄（鉄製）と刀身とが接合され一体となるよう作られている（共鉄造り）。
別称に「'''衛府の太刀'''」がある。また一部では『白河上皇高野御幸記（高野行幸記）』の中で「'''俘囚野剣'''」とも呼ばれているとされるが、原本である高野山西南院所蔵『寛治二年白河上皇高野御幸記』の中に俘囚野剣という語の記述は確認出来ない{{refnest|group=原|name=高野山西南院所蔵『寛治二年白河上皇高野御幸記』|高野山西南院所蔵『寛治二年白河上皇高野御幸記』}}
== 成立過程 == 毛抜形太刀の直接的な起源は、[[古墳時代]]、[[東北地方]]の[[蝦夷]]が用いてきた[[蕨手刀]]である。最初期の蕨手刀は[[直刀]]だったが、改良により、突き刺すのではなく騎乗にて振り下ろして斬る使い方（疾駆斬撃戦）に適すように、次第に柄および刃が反り、彎刀に近づいた。
[[朝廷 (日本)|朝廷]]の律令軍によって東北地方が制圧支配されると、蝦夷の騎乗武器・戦法の特徴が和人へ伝わり、蕨手刀が段階的に改良発展された結果として、下記に述べるように日本刀の原型とされる毛抜形太刀へ至った。
まず、蕨手刀の柄に透かしをつけた'''[[毛抜形蕨手刀]]'''に改良された。これは出土状況などから[[9世紀]]初めに蝦夷自身の手によって改良されたものと見られている。この柄の透かしによって、握りやすくなり、柄と刀が一体であった蕨手刀の弱点である斬撃時の衝撃を緩和させることに成功している。
さらに、毛抜形蕨手刀の柄頭から特徴的であった蕨形の装飾が消えて'''[[毛抜形刀]]'''となる。柄頭の装飾が消えたことからも、実用性に重きが置かれていく過程が分かる。この毛抜形刀についても、蝦夷が9世紀末までに開発したものと考えられている。
[[舞草]]（[[一関市]]）にこの毛抜形刀の鍛冶集団が生まれ、舞草鍛冶、舞草刀と呼ばれた。
最古のものは、[[長野県]][[塩尻市]][[宗賀 (塩尻市)|宗賀]]で出土したもので、[[10世紀]]のものと推定されている。出土刀も、神社に奉納されていた伝世刀も、全て関東以西である。
=== 誕生 === さらに、毛抜形刀から、より長い刃長を持つ毛抜形太刀が生まれた<ref>9世紀末から10世紀前半の東国の乱の中で起こったものではないかと下向井龍彦（[[広島大学]]大学院教育学研究科教授）は考察している。</ref>。この毛抜形太刀は急速に普及し、[[衛府]]官人（天皇親衛隊幹部）の制式太刀として採用されるに至っている。
この太刀の登場により、律令的戦術から脱した武人・武官達は、中世の武士の原型を作ることとなる。
== 文化財 == ; [[ファイル:Kin-ji raden kenuki-gata tachi.jpg|サムネイル|400x400ピクセル|金地螺鈿毛抜形太刀（春日大社蔵、国宝）]][[毛抜形太刀 (重要文化財工第1354号)|毛抜形太刀]] : [[伊勢神宮]]蔵。重要文化財。詳細は当該項目を参照。 ; [[毛抜形太刀〈無銘（伝藤原秀郷奉納）/〉附梨子地桐紋蒔絵鞘]] : [[宝厳寺]]蔵。重要文化財。詳細は当該項目を参照。 ; [[毛抜形太刀〈無銘/〉]] : [[太宰府天満宮]]蔵。重要文化財。詳細は当該項目を参照。 ; 金地螺鈿毛抜形太刀（きんじらでん けぬきがたたち） : 現存する毛抜形太刀の中でも最高作とされ<!-- 『日本の美術 刀剣』第六号 編集 佐藤寒山 至文堂 1966年 p.86 -->、奈良[[春日大社]]蔵。ただし、深く錆つき、刀身は抜けないとされる。鞘には[[漆工#加飾による分類|螺鈿]]と[[漆工#加飾による分類|蒔絵]]が施されている。 : 平成27年（[[2015年]]）から翌28年にかけての春日大社第60次式年造替に合わせて同太刀の復元が試みられ、その様子は平成28年12月にNHKドキュメンタリーとして放送された<ref>[http://www.nhk.or.jp/docudocu/program/92393/2393116/ 春日大社 よみがえる黄金の太刀～平安の名宝に秘められた技～]</ref>。
== 備考 == * この他、[[竹生島]]にも平家の公達[[平経盛|経盛]]の所用とされるものが奉納されているが、これも焼け身である<!-- 同『日本美術 刀剣』 至文堂 -->。 * 赤地錦包毛抜形太刀のレプリカは、[[東京国立博物館]]において見られる<!-- 『特別展 誕生武蔵武士』、同『日本美術 刀剣』至文堂 -->。 * [[宮城学院女子大学]]名誉教授の佐々木忠慧が1990年2月に発表した説として、[[清少納言]]著の『[[枕草子]]』内の「たちはたまつくり」の一文を「太刀は玉造鍛冶（現西大崎地区）の作がよい」と解釈したものがある。これは「舞草（もくさ）刀研究会」が明らかにした玉造鍛冶像をヒントにした説であり、『枕草子』が成立した時期（10世紀末）、舞草鍛冶の鍛えた刀の出来栄えが宮廷で話題となり、清少納言が簡潔に書きまとめたとする説である。舞草刀とは極初期の日本刀と分類される刀であり、玉造鍛冶は現在の[[一関市]]を中心として活動した舞草鍛冶が俘囚として玉造郡衙（現[[大崎市]]、旧[[岩出山町]]西大崎地区）に移された（岩手から宮城へ南下した）集団と考えられている<!-- 週刊『再現日本史 平安①794~805 桓武天皇の決断 平安京に遷都!』 講談社 2003年 p.38参考 -->。いずれにしても、日本刀の成立には蝦夷鍛冶の技術力に因があることは確かである。 * 考古学研究者の津野仁は、共鉄柄で柄頭が方形の刀に対して、「方頭共鉄柄刀」という呼称を提唱し、木柄刀との系統的な発展の違いと関連について、近年、論文を出している（津野仁 『日本刀の成立過程 - 木柄刀と古代刀の変遷 -』 2010年）。 * 日本陸海軍の[[元帥杖|元帥刀]]は毛抜形太刀を模した様式であった。
==脚注== {{脚注ヘルプ}}
=== 原典 === {{Reflist|group="原"}}
=== 出典 === {{Reflist}}
== 参考文献 == * 日本の歴史07 『武士の成長と院政』 2001年 下向井龍彦 [[講談社]] ISBN 4-06-268907-3 * 『-特別展- 誕生 武蔵武士』 埼玉県立歴史と民俗の博物館 2009年
{{デフォルトソート:けぬきかたたち}} [[Category:日本刀]]

revision.text:
'''Kenukigata Tachi''' (毛抜形太刀) refers to a style of [[tachi]] (long sword) that emerged during the mid-[[Heian period]]. It is considered the prototype of the [[Japanese sword]] (nihonto). The sword is crafted as a single unit, with the handle (made of iron) and blade forged together (referred to as kyōtetsuzukuri).
The sword is also known as the "'''Efu no Tachi'''” (衛府の太刀). Some sources claim it is referred to as "'''Fushū no Ken'''” (俘囚野剣) in the {{ill|Shirakawa Jōkō Kōya Gokōki|ja|白河上皇高野御幸記|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}; however, no mention of this term is found in the original text housed at Kōyasan Seinōin, {{Reflist|group="Primary"}}.
== Formation == The direct origin of the Kenukigata Tachi is traced to the [[Kofun period]]'s {{ill|Warabite-tō|ja|蕨手刀|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, used by the {{ill|Emishi|ja|蝦夷|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} of northeastern Japan. Initially, the warabite-tō were straight-bladed swords (chokutō), but their design evolved into curved blades better suited for mounted combat (jikku zangeki-sen) by striking downward while riding.
When the [[Imperial Court]] subdued the northeastern region, the Emishi's weapons and tactics influenced the Japanese people. Over time, the warabite-tō underwent refinements that eventually culminated in the Kenukigata Tachi, widely regarded as the prototype of the Japanese sword.
The transitional design, known as the "Kenukigata Warabite-tō," added an openwork pattern to the handle, improving grip and mitigating the shock of impact during strikes. This modification likely originated with the Emishi in the early 9th century. Subsequently, the decorative "warabi" motif on the handle disappeared, leading to the "Kenukigata-tō," which further emphasized functionality. The Kenukigata-tō is thought to have been developed by the Emishi by the late 9th century.
The {{ill|Moegusa|ja|舞草|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} area (modern-day [[Ichinoseki City]]) became a hub for crafting these swords, and the smiths there were referred to as the Moegusa Forgers (舞草鍛冶).
The oldest example of the Kenukigata Tachi was unearthed in [[Soga]], {{ill|Shiojiri|ja|塩尻市|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, [[Nagano Prefecture]], and is estimated to date back to the 10th century. Archaeological finds and preserved examples are limited to areas west of the Kantō region.
=== Emergence === The Kenukigata Tachi evolved into a longer sword during the late 9th to early 10th century, coinciding with regional conflicts in the East. It rapidly gained popularity and was adopted as a standard ceremonial weapon for {{ill|Royal guard|ja|衛府|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} (imperial guards). The emergence of this sword marked a transition from Ritsuryō-era tactics and contributed to the formation of Japan's medieval warrior class.
== Cultural Properties == ; {{ill|Kenukigata Tachi (Important Cultural Property No. 1354)|ja|毛抜形太刀 (重要文化財工第1354号)|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} : Housed at [[Ise Shrine]], designated as an Important Cultural Property. ; {{ill|Kenukigata Tachi (Hōgon-ji)|ja|毛抜形太刀〈無銘（伝Fujiwara no Hidesato奉納）/〉附梨子地桐紋蒔絵鞘|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} : Housed at [[Hōgon-ji Temple]], designated as an Important Cultural Property. ; {{ill|Kenukigata Tachi (Dazaifu Tenman-gū)|ja|毛抜形太刀〈無銘/〉|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} : Housed at [[Dazaifu Tenman-gū Shrine]], designated as an Important Cultural Property. ; Gold-Inlaid Lacquer Kenukigata Tachi : Considered the finest example among surviving Kenukigata Tachi, housed at {{ill|Kasuga-taisha|ja|春日大社|1=Kasuga-taisha|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} Shrine in [[Nara]]. Despite severe rust, the scabbard features intricate {{ill|raden|ja|螺鈿|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} and lacquerwork. Restoration efforts during the shrine's 60th ceremonial rebuilding (2015–2016) were featured in a 2016 NHK documentary.<ref>[http://www.nhk.or.jp/docudocu/program/92393/2393pp6/ "Kasuga-taisha: Reviving a Golden Sword—Secrets of a Heian-Era Treasure"] (NHK).</ref>
== Notes == * A Kenukigata Tachi attributed to {{ill|Taira no Tsunemori|ja|平経盛|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} is housed at [[Chikubu Island]], though it is in poor condition. * A replica of a crimson Kenukigata Tachi can be viewed at the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. * {{ill|Sasaki Tadakiyo|ja|佐々木忠慧|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}}, a professor at [[Miyagi Gakuin Women's University]], suggested in 1990 that the phrase "tachihatamatsukuri" in {{ill|The Pillow Book|ja|枕草子|12=draft|12=draft|12=draft}} refers to swords crafted by the Moegusa smiths. This interpretation highlights the importance of Emishi craftsmanship in the development of Japanese swords.
== Footnotes ==
{{Reflist|group="Primary"}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * ''The Growth of Warriors and the Insei Period,'' Ryūhiko Shimomukai, 2001, [[Kōdansha]]. ISBN 4-06-268907-3. * ''-Special Exhibition- Birth of Musashi Bushi,'' Saitama Prefectural Museum of History and Folklore, 2009.
{{translated page|ja|毛抜形太刀|version=103649806|comment=Imported full ja history}}

revision.comment: Text replacement - "12=draft" to "qq=draft"
revision.text:
'''Kenukigata Tachi''' (毛抜形太刀) refers to a style of [[tachi]] (long sword) that emerged during the mid-[[Heian period]]. It is considered the prototype of the [[Japanese sword]] (nihonto). The sword is crafted as a single unit, with the handle (made of iron) and blade forged together (referred to as kyōtetsuzukuri).
The sword is also known as the "'''Efu no Tachi'''” (衛府の太刀). Some sources claim it is referred to as "'''Fushū no Ken'''” (俘囚野剣) in the {{ill|Shirakawa Jōkō Kōya Gokōki|ja|白河上皇高野御幸記|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}; however, no mention of this term is found in the original text housed at Kōyasan Seinōin, {{Reflist|group="Primary"}}.
== Formation == The direct origin of the Kenukigata Tachi is traced to the [[Kofun period]]'s {{ill|Warabite-tō|ja|蕨手刀|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, used by the {{ill|Emishi|ja|蝦夷|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} of northeastern Japan. Initially, the warabite-tō were straight-bladed swords (chokutō), but their design evolved into curved blades better suited for mounted combat (jikku zangeki-sen) by striking downward while riding.
When the [[Imperial Court]] subdued the northeastern region, the Emishi's weapons and tactics influenced the Japanese people. Over time, the warabite-tō underwent refinements that eventually culminated in the Kenukigata Tachi, widely regarded as the prototype of the Japanese sword.
The transitional design, known as the "Kenukigata Warabite-tō," added an openwork pattern to the handle, improving grip and mitigating the shock of impact during strikes. This modification likely originated with the Emishi in the early 9th century. Subsequently, the decorative "warabi" motif on the handle disappeared, leading to the "Kenukigata-tō," which further emphasized functionality. The Kenukigata-tō is thought to have been developed by the Emishi by the late 9th century.
The {{ill|Moegusa|ja|舞草|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} area (modern-day [[Ichinoseki City]]) became a hub for crafting these swords, and the smiths there were referred to as the Moegusa Forgers (舞草鍛冶).
The oldest example of the Kenukigata Tachi was unearthed in [[Soga]], {{ill|Shiojiri|ja|塩尻市|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, [[Nagano Prefecture]], and is estimated to date back to the 10th century. Archaeological finds and preserved examples are limited to areas west of the Kantō region.
=== Emergence === The Kenukigata Tachi evolved into a longer sword during the late 9th to early 10th century, coinciding with regional conflicts in the East. It rapidly gained popularity and was adopted as a standard ceremonial weapon for {{ill|Royal guard|ja|衛府|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} (imperial guards). The emergence of this sword marked a transition from Ritsuryō-era tactics and contributed to the formation of Japan's medieval warrior class.
== Cultural Properties == ; {{ill|Kenukigata Tachi (Important Cultural Property No. 1354)|ja|毛抜形太刀 (重要文化財工第1354号)|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} : Housed at [[Ise Shrine]], designated as an Important Cultural Property. ; {{ill|Kenukigata Tachi (Hōgon-ji)|ja|毛抜形太刀〈無銘（伝Fujiwara no Hidesato奉納）/〉附梨子地桐紋蒔絵鞘|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} : Housed at [[Hōgon-ji Temple]], designated as an Important Cultural Property. ; {{ill|Kenukigata Tachi (Dazaifu Tenman-gū)|ja|毛抜形太刀〈無銘/〉|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} : Housed at [[Dazaifu Tenman-gū Shrine]], designated as an Important Cultural Property. ; Gold-Inlaid Lacquer Kenukigata Tachi : Considered the finest example among surviving Kenukigata Tachi, housed at {{ill|Kasuga-taisha|ja|春日大社|1=Kasuga-taisha|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} Shrine in [[Nara]]. Despite severe rust, the scabbard features intricate {{ill|raden|ja|螺鈿|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} and lacquerwork. Restoration efforts during the shrine's 60th ceremonial rebuilding (2015–2016) were featured in a 2016 NHK documentary.<ref>[http://www.nhk.or.jp/docudocu/program/92393/2393pp6/ "Kasuga-taisha: Reviving a Golden Sword—Secrets of a Heian-Era Treasure"] (NHK).</ref>
== Notes == * A Kenukigata Tachi attributed to {{ill|Taira no Tsunemori|ja|平経盛|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} is housed at [[Chikubu Island]], though it is in poor condition. * A replica of a crimson Kenukigata Tachi can be viewed at the [[Tokyo National Museum]]. * {{ill|Sasaki Tadakiyo|ja|佐々木忠慧|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, a professor at [[Miyagi Gakuin Women's University]], suggested in 1990 that the phrase "tachihatamatsukuri" in {{ill|The Pillow Book|ja|枕草子|qq=draft|qq=draft|qq=draft}} refers to swords crafted by the Moegusa smiths. This interpretation highlights the importance of Emishi craftsmanship in the development of Japanese swords.
== Footnotes ==
{{Reflist|group="Primary"}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * ''The Growth of Warriors and the Insei Period,'' Ryūhiko Shimomukai, 2001, [[Kōdansha]]. ISBN 4-06-268907-3. * ''-Special Exhibition- Birth of Musashi Bushi,'' Saitama Prefectural Museum of History and Folklore, 2009.
{{translated page|ja|毛抜形太刀|version=103649806|comment=Imported full ja history}}
