<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Kenukigata tachi]][[ja:毛抜形太刀]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11547735]] Wikidata name: kenukigata tachi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:29, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11547735]] Wikidata name: kenukigata tachi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:09, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Kenukigata Tachi''' (毛抜形太刀) refers to a style of [[tachi]] (long sword) that emerged during the mid-[[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]. It is considered the prototype of the [[Japanese sword]] (nihonto). The sword is crafted as a single unit, with the handle (made of iron) and blade forged together (referred to as kyōtetsuzukuri).
The sword is also known as the "'''Efu no Tachi'''” (衛府の太刀). Some sources claim it is referred to as "'''Fushū no Ken'''” (俘囚野剣) in the {{ill|Shirakawa Jōkō Kōya Gokōki|ja|白河上皇高野御幸記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirakawa Jōkō Kōya Gokōki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}; however, no mention of this term is found in the original text housed at Kōyasan Seinōin, {{Reflist|group="Primary"}}.
== Formation == The direct origin of the Kenukigata Tachi is traced to the [[Kofun period]]'s {{ill|Warabite-tō|ja|蕨手刀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Warabite-tō|1=warabitetō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11621998}}, used by the [[:en:Emishi|Emishi]] of northeastern Japan. Initially, the warabite-tō were straight-bladed swords (chokutō), but their design evolved into curved blades better suited for mounted combat (jikku zangeki-sen) by striking downward while riding.
When the [[:en:Imperial Court|Imperial Court]] subdued the northeastern region, the Emishi's weapons and tactics influenced the Japanese people. Over time, the warabite-tō underwent refinements that eventually culminated in the Kenukigata Tachi, widely regarded as the prototype of the Japanese sword.
The transitional design, known as the "Kenukigata Warabite-tō," added an openwork pattern to the handle, improving grip and mitigating the shock of impact during strikes. This modification likely originated with the Emishi in the early 9th century. Subsequently, the decorative "warabi" motif on the handle disappeared, leading to the "Kenukigata-tō," which further emphasized functionality. The Kenukigata-tō is thought to have been developed by the Emishi by the late 9th century.
The {{ill|Moegusa|ja|舞草|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moegusa|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 一関市|1=Ichinoseki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q390817}} area (modern-day [[Ichinoseki City]]) became a hub for crafting these swords, and the smiths there were referred to as the Moegusa Forgers (舞草鍛冶).
The oldest example of the Kenukigata Tachi was unearthed in [[:en:Soga|Soga]], [[:en:Shiojiri, Nagano|Shiojiri]], [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]], and is estimated to date back to the 10th century. Archaeological finds and preserved examples are limited to areas west of the Kantō region.
=== Emergence === The Kenukigata Tachi evolved into a longer sword during the late 9th to early 10th century, coinciding with regional conflicts in the East. It rapidly gained popularity and was adopted as a standard ceremonial weapon for {{ill|Royal guard|ja|衛府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Royal guard|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近衛兵|1=royal guard|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q971045}} (imperial guards). The emergence of this sword marked a transition from Ritsuryō-era tactics and contributed to the formation of Japan's medieval warrior class.
== Cultural Properties == ; {{ill|Kenukigata Tachi (Important Cultural Property No. 1354)|ja|毛抜形太刀 (重要文化財工第1354号)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenukigata Tachi (Important Cultural Property No. 1354)|1=Kenuki-gata tachi at Ise Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q28688501}} : Housed at [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Shrine]], designated as an Important Cultural Property. ; {{ill|Kenukigata Tachi (Hōgon-ji)|ja|毛抜形太刀〈無銘（伝Fujiwara no Hidesato奉納）/〉附梨子地桐紋蒔絵鞘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenukigata Tachi (Hōgon-ji)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} : Housed at [[Hōgon-ji Temple]], designated as an Important Cultural Property. ; {{ill|Kenukigata Tachi (Dazaifu Tenman-gū)|ja|毛抜形太刀〈無銘/〉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenukigata Tachi (Dazaifu Tenman-gū)|1=Kenukigata Tachi (Dazaifu Tenman-gū)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q28690704}} : Housed at [[Dazaifu Tenman-gū Shrine]], designated as an Important Cultural Property. ; Gold-Inlaid Lacquer Kenukigata Tachi : Considered the finest example among surviving Kenukigata Tachi, housed at [[:en:Kasuga-taisha|Kasuga-taisha]] Shrine in [[Nara]]. Despite severe rust, the scabbard features intricate [[:en:Raden|raden]] and lacquerwork. Restoration efforts during the shrine's 60th ceremonial rebuilding (2015–2016) were featured in a 2016 NHK documentary.<ref>[http://www.nhk.or.jp/docudocu/program/92393/2393pp6/ "Kasuga-taisha: Reviving a Golden Sword—Secrets of a Heian-Era Treasure"] (NHK).</ref>
== Notes == * A Kenukigata Tachi attributed to [[:en:Taira no Tsunemori|Taira no Tsunemori]] is housed at [[:en:Chikubu Island|Chikubu Island]], though it is in poor condition. * A replica of a crimson Kenukigata Tachi can be viewed at the [[:en:Tokyo National Museum|Tokyo National Museum]]. * {{ill|Sasaki Tadakiyo|ja|佐々木忠慧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasaki Tadakiyo|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a professor at [[:en:Miyagi Gakuin Women's University|Miyagi Gakuin Women's University]], suggested in 1990 that the phrase "tachihatamatsukuri" in [[:en:The Pillow Book|The Pillow Book]] refers to swords crafted by the Moegusa smiths. This interpretation highlights the importance of Emishi craftsmanship in the development of Japanese swords.
== Footnotes ==
{{Reflist|group="Primary"}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * ''The Growth of Warriors and the Insei Period,'' Ryūhiko Shimomukai, 2001, [[:en:Kōdansha|Kōdansha]]. ISBN 4-06-268907-3. * ''-Special Exhibition- Birth of Musashi Bushi,'' Saitama Prefectural Museum of History and Folklore, 2009.

It rapidly gained popularity and was adopted as a standard ceremonial weapon for {{ill|Royal guard|ja|衛府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Royal guard|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近衛兵|1=royal guard|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q971045}} (imperial guards). The emergence of this sword marked a transition from Ritsuryō-era tactics and contributed to the formation of Japan's medieval warrior class.
== Cultural Properties == ; {{ill|Kenukigata Tachi (Important Cultural Property No. 1354)|ja|毛抜形太刀 (重要文化財工第1354号)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenukigata Tachi (Important Cultural Property No. 1354)|1=Kenuki-gata tachi at Ise Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q28688501}} : Housed at [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Shrine]], designated as an Important Cultural Property. ; {{ill|Kenukigata Tachi (Hōgon-ji)|ja|毛抜形太刀〈無銘（伝Fujiwara no Hidesato奉納）/〉附梨子地桐紋蒔絵鞘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenukigata Tachi (Hōgon-ji)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} : Housed at [[Hōgon-ji Temple]], designated as an Important Cultural Property. ; {{ill|Kenukigata Tachi (Dazaifu Tenman-gū)|ja|毛抜形太刀〈無銘/〉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenukigata Tachi (Dazaifu Tenman-gū)|1=Kenukigata Tachi (Dazaifu Tenman-gū)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q28690704}} : Housed at [[Dazaifu Tenman-gū Shrine]], designated as an Important Cultural Property. ; Gold-Inlaid Lacquer Kenukigata Tachi : Considered the finest example among surviving Kenukigata Tachi, housed at [[:en:Kasuga-taisha|Kasuga-taisha]] Shrine in [[Nara]]. Despite severe rust, the scabbard features intricate [[:en:Raden|raden]] and lacquerwork. Restoration efforts during the shrine's 60th ceremonial rebuilding (2015–2016) were featured in a 2016 NHK documentary.<ref>[http://www.nhk.or.jp/docudocu/program/92393/2393pp6/ "Kasuga-taisha: Reviving a Golden Sword—Secrets of a Heian-Era Treasure"] (NHK).</ref>
== Notes == * A Kenukigata Tachi attributed to [[:en:Taira no Tsunemori|Taira no Tsunemori]] is housed at [[:en:Chikubu Island|Chikubu Island]], though it is in poor condition. * A replica of a crimson Kenukigata Tachi can be viewed at the [[:en:Tokyo National Museum|Tokyo National Museum]]. * {{ill|Sasaki Tadakiyo|ja|佐々木忠慧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasaki Tadakiyo|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a professor at [[:en:Miyagi Gakuin Women's University|Miyagi Gakuin Women's University]], suggested in 1990 that the phrase "tachihatamatsukuri" in [[:en:The Pillow Book|The Pillow Book]] refers to swords crafted by the Moegusa smiths. This interpretation highlights the importance of Emishi craftsmanship in the development of Japanese swords.
== Footnotes ==
{{Reflist|group="Primary"}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * ''The Growth of Warriors and the Insei Period,'' Ryūhiko Shimomukai, 2001, [[:en:Kōdansha|Kōdansha]]. ISBN 4-06-268907-3. * ''-Special Exhibition- Birth of Musashi Bushi,'' Saitama Prefectural Museum of History and Folklore, 2009.

revision.comment: Removing from [[Category:Qq]] using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Kenukigata tachi]][[ja:毛抜形太刀]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11547735]] Wikidata name: kenukigata tachi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:29, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11547735]] Wikidata name: kenukigata tachi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:09, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Kenukigata Tachi''' (毛抜形太刀) refers to a style of [[tachi]] (long sword) that emerged during the mid-[[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]. It is considered the prototype of the [[Japanese sword]] (nihonto). The sword is crafted as a single unit, with the handle (made of iron) and blade forged together (referred to as kyōtetsuzukuri).
The sword is also known as the "'''Efu no Tachi'''” (衛府の太刀). Some sources claim it is referred to as "'''Fushū no Ken'''” (俘囚野剣) in the {{ill|Shirakawa Jōkō Kōya Gokōki|ja|白河上皇高野御幸記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirakawa Jōkō Kōya Gokōki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}; however, no mention of this term is found in the original text housed at Kōyasan Seinōin, {{Reflist|group="Primary"}}.
== Formation == The direct origin of the Kenukigata Tachi is traced to the [[Kofun period]]'s {{ill|Warabite-tō|ja|蕨手刀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Warabite-tō|1=warabitetō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11621998}}, used by the [[:en:Emishi|Emishi]] of northeastern Japan. Initially, the warabite-tō were straight-bladed swords (chokutō), but their design evolved into curved blades better suited for mounted combat (jikku zangeki-sen) by striking downward while riding.
When the [[:en:Imperial Court|Imperial Court]] subdued the northeastern region, the Emishi's weapons and tactics influenced the Japanese people. Over time, the warabite-tō underwent refinements that eventually culminated in the Kenukigata Tachi, widely regarded as the prototype of the Japanese sword.
The transitional design, known as the "Kenukigata Warabite-tō," added an openwork pattern to the handle, improving grip and mitigating the shock of impact during strikes. This modification likely originated with the Emishi in the early 9th century. Subsequently, the decorative "warabi" motif on the handle disappeared, leading to the "Kenukigata-tō," which further emphasized functionality. The Kenukigata-tō is thought to have been developed by the Emishi by the late 9th century.
The {{ill|Moegusa|ja|舞草|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moegusa|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 一関市|1=Ichinoseki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q390817}} area (modern-day [[Ichinoseki City]]) became a hub for crafting these swords, and the smiths there were referred to as the Moegusa Forgers (舞草鍛冶).
The oldest example of the Kenukigata Tachi was unearthed in [[:en:Soga|Soga]], [[:en:Shiojiri, Nagano|Shiojiri]], [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]], and is estimated to date back to the 10th century. Archaeological finds and preserved examples are limited to areas west of the Kantō region.
=== Emergence === The Kenukigata Tachi evolved into a longer sword during the late 9th to early 10th century, coinciding with regional conflicts in the East. It rapidly gained popularity and was adopted as a standard ceremonial weapon for {{ill|Royal guard|ja|衛府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Royal guard|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近衛兵|1=royal guard|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q971045}} (imperial guards). The emergence of this sword marked a transition from Ritsuryō-era tactics and contributed to the formation of Japan's medieval warrior class.
== Cultural Properties == ; {{ill|Kenukigata Tachi (Important Cultural Property No. 1354)|ja|毛抜形太刀 (重要文化財工第1354号)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenukigata Tachi (Important Cultural Property No. 1354)|1=Kenuki-gata tachi at Ise Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q28688501}} : Housed at [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Shrine]], designated as an Important Cultural Property. ; {{ill|Kenukigata Tachi (Hōgon-ji)|ja|毛抜形太刀〈無銘（伝Fujiwara no Hidesato奉納）/〉附梨子地桐紋蒔絵鞘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenukigata Tachi (Hōgon-ji)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} : Housed at [[Hōgon-ji Temple]], designated as an Important Cultural Property. ; {{ill|Kenukigata Tachi (Dazaifu Tenman-gū)|ja|毛抜形太刀〈無銘/〉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenukigata Tachi (Dazaifu Tenman-gū)|1=Kenukigata Tachi (Dazaifu Tenman-gū)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q28690704}} : Housed at [[Dazaifu Tenman-gū Shrine]], designated as an Important Cultural Property. ; Gold-Inlaid Lacquer Kenukigata Tachi : Considered the finest example among surviving Kenukigata Tachi, housed at [[:en:Kasuga-taisha|Kasuga-taisha]] Shrine in [[Nara]]. Despite severe rust, the scabbard features intricate [[:en:Raden|raden]] and lacquerwork. Restoration efforts during the shrine's 60th ceremonial rebuilding (2015–2016) were featured in a 2016 NHK documentary.<ref>[http://www.nhk.or.jp/docudocu/program/92393/2393pp6/ "Kasuga-taisha: Reviving a Golden Sword—Secrets of a Heian-Era Treasure"] (NHK).</ref>
== Notes == * A Kenukigata Tachi attributed to [[:en:Taira no Tsunemori|Taira no Tsunemori]] is housed at [[:en:Chikubu Island|Chikubu Island]], though it is in poor condition. * A replica of a crimson Kenukigata Tachi can be viewed at the [[:en:Tokyo National Museum|Tokyo National Museum]]. * {{ill|Sasaki Tadakiyo|ja|佐々木忠慧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasaki Tadakiyo|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a professor at [[:en:Miyagi Gakuin Women's University|Miyagi Gakuin Women's University]], suggested in 1990 that the phrase "tachihatamatsukuri" in [[:en:The Pillow Book|The Pillow Book]] refers to swords crafted by the Moegusa smiths. This interpretation highlights the importance of Emishi craftsmanship in the development of Japanese swords.
== Footnotes ==
{{Reflist|group="Primary"}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * ''The Growth of Warriors and the Insei Period,'' Ryūhiko Shimomukai, 2001, [[:en:Kōdansha|Kōdansha]]. ISBN 4-06-268907-3. * ''-Special Exhibition- Birth of Musashi Bushi,'' Saitama Prefectural Museum of History and Folklore, 2009.

[[ja:毛抜形太刀]]
<!--Mounuki-type swords--><!--Mounuki-type swords--><!--Mounuki-type swords--><!--Mounuki-type swords--><!--Mounuki-type swords--><!--Mounuki-type swords--> <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{wikidata link|Q11547735}} [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]]

revision.comment: Text replacement - "<!--Doing this to bump these pages because my history retrieval attempts have led to category caching errors-->" to ""
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Kenukigata tachi]][[ja:毛抜形太刀]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[commons:Category:Kenukigata tachi]][[ja:毛抜形太刀]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Kenukigata tachi]][[ja:毛抜形太刀]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11547735]] Wikidata name: kenukigata tachi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:29, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11547735]] Wikidata name: kenukigata tachi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:09, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Kenukigata Tachi''' (毛抜形太刀) refers to a style of [[tachi]] (long sword) that emerged during the mid-[[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]. It is considered the prototype of the [[Japanese sword]] (nihonto). The sword is crafted as a single unit, with the handle (made of iron) and blade forged together (referred to as kyōtetsuzukuri).
The sword is also known as the "'''Efu no Tachi'''” (衛府の太刀). Some sources claim it is referred to as "'''Fushū no Ken'''” (俘囚野剣) in the {{ill|Shirakawa Jōkō Kōya Gokōki|ja|白河上皇高野御幸記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirakawa Jōkō Kōya Gokōki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}; however, no mention of this term is found in the original text housed at Kōyasan Seinōin, {{Reflist|group="Primary"}}.
== Formation == The direct origin of the Kenukigata Tachi is traced to the [[Kofun period]]'s {{ill|Warabite-tō|ja|蕨手刀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Warabite-tō|1=warabitetō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11621998}}, used by the [[:en:Emishi|Emishi]] of northeastern Japan. Initially, the warabite-tō were straight-bladed swords (chokutō), but their design evolved into curved blades better suited for mounted combat (jikku zangeki-sen) by striking downward while riding.
When the [[:en:Imperial Court|Imperial Court]] subdued the northeastern region, the Emishi's weapons and tactics influenced the Japanese people. Over time, the warabite-tō underwent refinements that eventually culminated in the Kenukigata Tachi, widely regarded as the prototype of the Japanese sword.
The transitional design, known as the "Kenukigata Warabite-tō," added an openwork pattern to the handle, improving grip and mitigating the shock of impact during strikes. This modification likely originated with the Emishi in the early 9th century. Subsequently, the decorative "warabi" motif on the handle disappeared, leading to the "Kenukigata-tō," which further emphasized functionality. The Kenukigata-tō is thought to have been developed by the Emishi by the late 9th century.
The {{ill|Moegusa|ja|舞草|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moegusa|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 一関市|1=Ichinoseki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q390817}} area (modern-day [[Ichinoseki City]]) became a hub for crafting these swords, and the smiths there were referred to as the Moegusa Forgers (舞草鍛冶).
The oldest example of the Kenukigata Tachi was unearthed in [[:en:Soga|Soga]], [[:en:Shiojiri, Nagano|Shiojiri]], [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]], and is estimated to date back to the 10th century. Archaeological finds and preserved examples are limited to areas west of the Kantō region.
=== Emergence === The Kenukigata Tachi evolved into a longer sword during the late 9th to early 10th century, coinciding with regional conflicts in the East. It rapidly gained popularity and was adopted as a standard ceremonial weapon for {{ill|Royal guard|ja|衛府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Royal guard|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近衛兵|1=royal guard|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q971045}} (imperial guards). The emergence of this sword marked a transition from Ritsuryō-era tactics and contributed to the formation of Japan's medieval warrior class.
== Cultural Properties == ; {{ill|Kenukigata Tachi (Important Cultural Property No. 1354)|ja|毛抜形太刀 (重要文化財工第1354号)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenukigata Tachi (Important Cultural Property No. 1354)|1=Kenuki-gata tachi at Ise Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q28688501}} : Housed at [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Shrine]], designated as an Important Cultural Property. ; {{ill|Kenukigata Tachi (Hōgon-ji)|ja|毛抜形太刀〈無銘（伝Fujiwara no Hidesato奉納）/〉附梨子地桐紋蒔絵鞘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenukigata Tachi (Hōgon-ji)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} : Housed at [[Hōgon-ji Temple]], designated as an Important Cultural Property. ; {{ill|Kenukigata Tachi (Dazaifu Tenman-gū)|ja|毛抜形太刀〈無銘/〉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenukigata Tachi (Dazaifu Tenman-gū)|1=Kenukigata Tachi (Dazaifu Tenman-gū)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q28690704}} : Housed at [[Dazaifu Tenman-gū Shrine]], designated as an Important Cultural Property. ; Gold-Inlaid Lacquer Kenukigata Tachi : Considered the finest example among surviving Kenukigata Tachi, housed at [[:en:Kasuga-taisha|Kasuga-taisha]] Shrine in [[Nara]]. Despite severe rust, the scabbard features intricate [[:en:Raden|raden]] and lacquerwork. Restoration efforts during the shrine's 60th ceremonial rebuilding (2015–2016) were featured in a 2016 NHK documentary.<ref>[http://www.nhk.or.jp/docudocu/program/92393/2393pp6/ "Kasuga-taisha: Reviving a Golden Sword—Secrets of a Heian-Era Treasure"] (NHK).</ref>
== Notes == * A Kenukigata Tachi attributed to [[:en:Taira no Tsunemori|Taira no Tsunemori]] is housed at [[:en:Chikubu Island|Chikubu Island]], though it is in poor condition. * A replica of a crimson Kenukigata Tachi can be viewed at the [[:en:Tokyo National Museum|Tokyo National Museum]]. * {{ill|Sasaki Tadakiyo|ja|佐々木忠慧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasaki Tadakiyo|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a professor at [[:en:Miyagi Gakuin Women's University|Miyagi Gakuin Women's University]], suggested in 1990 that the phrase "tachihatamatsukuri" in [[:en:The Pillow Book|The Pillow Book]] refers to swords crafted by the Moegusa smiths. This interpretation highlights the importance of Emishi craftsmanship in the development of Japanese swords.
== Footnotes ==
{{Reflist|group="Primary"}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * ''The Growth of Warriors and the Insei Period,'' Ryūhiko Shimomukai, 2001, [[:en:Kōdansha|Kōdansha]]. ISBN 4-06-268907-3. * ''-Special Exhibition- Birth of Musashi Bushi,'' Saitama Prefectural Museum of History and Folklore, 2009.

When the [[:en:Imperial Court|Imperial Court]] subdued the northeastern region, the Emishi's weapons and tactics influenced the Japanese people. The transitional design, known as the "Kenukigata Warabite-tō," added an openwork pattern to the handle, improving grip and mitigating the shock of impact during strikes. This modification likely originated with the Emishi in the early 9th century. Subsequently, the decorative "warabi" motif on the handle disappeared, leading to the "Kenukigata-tō," which further emphasized functionality. The Kenukigata-tō is thought to have been developed by the Emishi by the late 9th century.
The {{ill|Moegusa|ja|舞草|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moegusa|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 一関市|1=Ichinoseki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q390817}} area (modern-day [[Ichinoseki City]]) became a hub for crafting these swords, and the smiths there were referred to as the Moegusa Forgers (舞草鍛冶).
The oldest example of the Kenukigata Tachi was unearthed in [[:en:Soga|Soga]], [[:en:Shiojiri, Nagano|Shiojiri]], [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]], and is estimated to date back to the 10th century. Archaeological finds and preserved examples are limited to areas west of the Kantō region.

It rapidly gained popularity and was adopted as a standard ceremonial weapon for {{ill|Royal guard|ja|衛府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Royal guard|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近衛兵|1=royal guard|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q971045}} (imperial guards). The emergence of this sword marked a transition from Ritsuryō-era tactics and contributed to the formation of Japan's medieval warrior class.
== Cultural Properties == ; {{ill|Kenukigata Tachi (Important Cultural Property No. 1354)|ja|毛抜形太刀 (重要文化財工第1354号)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenukigata Tachi (Important Cultural Property No. 1354)|1=Kenuki-gata tachi at Ise Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q28688501}} : Housed at [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Shrine]], designated as an Important Cultural Property. ; {{ill|Kenukigata Tachi (Hōgon-ji)|ja|毛抜形太刀〈無銘（伝Fujiwara no Hidesato奉納）/〉附梨子地桐紋蒔絵鞘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenukigata Tachi (Hōgon-ji)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} : Housed at [[Hōgon-ji Temple]], designated as an Important Cultural Property. ; {{ill|Kenukigata Tachi (Dazaifu Tenman-gū)|ja|毛抜形太刀〈無銘/〉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenukigata Tachi (Dazaifu Tenman-gū)|1=Kenukigata Tachi (Dazaifu Tenman-gū)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q28690704}} : Housed at [[Dazaifu Tenman-gū Shrine]], designated as an Important Cultural Property. ; Gold-Inlaid Lacquer Kenukigata Tachi : Considered the finest example among surviving Kenukigata Tachi, housed at [[:en:Kasuga-taisha|Kasuga-taisha]] Shrine in [[Nara]]. Despite severe rust, the scabbard features intricate [[:en:Raden|raden]] and lacquerwork. Restoration efforts during the shrine's 60th ceremonial rebuilding (2015–2016) were featured in a 2016 NHK documentary.<ref>[http://www.nhk.or.jp/docudocu/program/92393/2393pp6/ "Kasuga-taisha: Reviving a Golden Sword—Secrets of a Heian-Era Treasure"] (NHK).</ref>

This interpretation highlights the importance of Emishi craftsmanship in the development of Japanese swords.
== Footnotes ==

This modification likely originated with the Emishi in the early 9th century. Subsequently, the decorative "warabi" motif on the handle disappeared, leading to the "Kenukigata-tō," which further emphasized functionality. The Kenukigata-tō is thought to have been developed by the Emishi by the late 9th century.
The {{ill|Moegusa|ja|舞草|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moegusa|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 一関市|1=Ichinoseki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q390817}} area (modern-day [[Ichinoseki City]]) became a hub for crafting these swords, and the smiths there were referred to as the Moegusa Forgers (舞草鍛冶).
The oldest example of the Kenukigata Tachi was unearthed in [[:en:Soga|Soga]], [[:en:Shiojiri, Nagano|Shiojiri]], [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]], and is estimated to date back to the 10th century. Archaeological finds and preserved examples are limited to areas west of the Kantō region.
=== Emergence === The Kenukigata Tachi evolved into a longer sword during the late 9th to early 10th century, coinciding with regional conflicts in the East. It rapidly gained popularity and was adopted as a standard ceremonial weapon for {{ill|Royal guard|ja|衛府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Royal guard|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近衛兵|1=royal guard|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q971045}} (imperial guards). The emergence of this sword marked a transition from Ritsuryō-era tactics and contributed to the formation of Japan's medieval warrior class.
== Cultural Properties == ; {{ill|Kenukigata Tachi (Important Cultural Property No. 1354)|ja|毛抜形太刀 (重要文化財工第1354号)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenukigata Tachi (Important Cultural Property No. 1354)|1=Kenuki-gata tachi at Ise Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q28688501}} : Housed at [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Shrine]], designated as an Important Cultural Property. ; {{ill|Kenukigata Tachi (Hōgon-ji)|ja|毛抜形太刀〈無銘（伝Fujiwara no Hidesato奉納）/〉附梨子地桐紋蒔絵鞘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenukigata Tachi (Hōgon-ji)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} : Housed at [[Hōgon-ji Temple]], designated as an Important Cultural Property. ; {{ill|Kenukigata Tachi (Dazaifu Tenman-gū)|ja|毛抜形太刀〈無銘/〉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenukigata Tachi (Dazaifu Tenman-gū)|1=Kenukigata Tachi (Dazaifu Tenman-gū)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q28690704}} : Housed at [[Dazaifu Tenman-gū Shrine]], designated as an Important Cultural Property. ; Gold-Inlaid Lacquer Kenukigata Tachi : Considered the finest example among surviving Kenukigata Tachi, housed at [[:en:Kasuga-taisha|Kasuga-taisha]] Shrine in [[Nara]]. Despite severe rust, the scabbard features intricate [[:en:Raden|raden]] and lacquerwork. Restoration efforts during the shrine's 60th ceremonial rebuilding (2015–2016) were featured in a 2016 NHK documentary.<ref>[http://www.nhk.or.jp/docudocu/program/92393/2393pp6/ "Kasuga-taisha: Reviving a Golden Sword—Secrets of a Heian-Era Treasure"] (NHK).</ref>
== Notes == * A Kenukigata Tachi attributed to [[:en:Taira no Tsunemori|Taira no Tsunemori]] is housed at [[:en:Chikubu Island|Chikubu Island]], though it is in poor condition. * A replica of a crimson Kenukigata Tachi can be viewed at the [[:en:Tokyo National Museum|Tokyo National Museum]]. * {{ill|Sasaki Tadakiyo|ja|佐々木忠慧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasaki Tadakiyo|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a professor at [[:en:Miyagi Gakuin Women's University|Miyagi Gakuin Women's University]], suggested in 1990 that the phrase "tachihatamatsukuri" in [[:en:The Pillow Book|The Pillow Book]] refers to swords crafted by the Moegusa smiths. This interpretation highlights the importance of Emishi craftsmanship in the development of Japanese swords.
== Footnotes ==
{{Reflist|group="Primary"}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * ''The Growth of Warriors and the Insei Period,'' Ryūhiko Shimomukai, 2001, [[:en:Kōdansha|Kōdansha]]. ISBN 4-06-268907-3. * ''-Special Exhibition- Birth of Musashi Bushi,'' Saitama Prefectural Museum of History and Folklore, 2009.

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kenukigata tachi]][[ja:毛抜形太刀]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kenukigata tachi]][[ja:毛抜形太刀]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Kenukigata tachi]][[ja:毛抜形太刀]] {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11547735]] Wikidata name: kenukigata tachi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:29, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11547735]] Wikidata name: kenukigata tachi [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 04:09, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} '''Kenukigata Tachi''' (毛抜形太刀) refers to a style of [[tachi]] (long sword) that emerged during the mid-[[:en:Heian period|Heian period]]. It is considered the prototype of the [[Japanese sword]] (nihonto). The sword is crafted as a single unit, with the handle (made of iron) and blade forged together (referred to as kyōtetsuzukuri).
The sword is also known as the "'''Efu no Tachi'''” (衛府の太刀). Some sources claim it is referred to as "'''Fushū no Ken'''” (俘囚野剣) in the {{ill|Shirakawa Jōkō Kōya Gokōki|ja|白河上皇高野御幸記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirakawa Jōkō Kōya Gokōki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}; however, no mention of this term is found in the original text housed at Kōyasan Seinōin, {{Reflist|group="Primary"}}.
== Formation == The direct origin of the Kenukigata Tachi is traced to the [[Kofun period]]'s {{ill|Warabite-tō|ja|蕨手刀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Warabite-tō|1=warabitetō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11621998}}, used by the [[:en:Emishi|Emishi]] of northeastern Japan. Initially, the warabite-tō were straight-bladed swords (chokutō), but their design evolved into curved blades better suited for mounted combat (jikku zangeki-sen) by striking downward while riding.
When the [[:en:Imperial Court|Imperial Court]] subdued the northeastern region, the Emishi's weapons and tactics influenced the Japanese people. Over time, the warabite-tō underwent refinements that eventually culminated in the Kenukigata Tachi, widely regarded as the prototype of the Japanese sword.
The transitional design, known as the "Kenukigata Warabite-tō," added an openwork pattern to the handle, improving grip and mitigating the shock of impact during strikes. This modification likely originated with the Emishi in the early 9th century. Subsequently, the decorative "warabi" motif on the handle disappeared, leading to the "Kenukigata-tō," which further emphasized functionality. The Kenukigata-tō is thought to have been developed by the Emishi by the late 9th century.
The {{ill|Moegusa|ja|舞草|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moegusa|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 一関市|1=Ichinoseki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q390817}} area (modern-day [[Ichinoseki City]]) became a hub for crafting these swords, and the smiths there were referred to as the Moegusa Forgers (舞草鍛冶).
The oldest example of the Kenukigata Tachi was unearthed in [[:en:Soga|Soga]], [[:en:Shiojiri, Nagano|Shiojiri]], [[:en:Nagano Prefecture|Nagano Prefecture]], and is estimated to date back to the 10th century. Archaeological finds and preserved examples are limited to areas west of the Kantō region.
=== Emergence === The Kenukigata Tachi evolved into a longer sword during the late 9th to early 10th century, coinciding with regional conflicts in the East. It rapidly gained popularity and was adopted as a standard ceremonial weapon for {{ill|Royal guard|ja|衛府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Royal guard|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近衛兵|1=royal guard|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q971045}} (imperial guards). The emergence of this sword marked a transition from Ritsuryō-era tactics and contributed to the formation of Japan's medieval warrior class.
== Cultural Properties == ; {{ill|Kenukigata Tachi (Important Cultural Property No. 1354)|ja|毛抜形太刀 (重要文化財工第1354号)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenukigata Tachi (Important Cultural Property No. 1354)|1=Kenuki-gata tachi at Ise Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q28688501}} : Housed at [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Shrine]], designated as an Important Cultural Property. ; {{ill|Kenukigata Tachi (Hōgon-ji)|ja|毛抜形太刀〈無銘（伝Fujiwara no Hidesato奉納）/〉附梨子地桐紋蒔絵鞘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenukigata Tachi (Hōgon-ji)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} : Housed at [[Hōgon-ji Temple]], designated as an Important Cultural Property. ; {{ill|Kenukigata Tachi (Dazaifu Tenman-gū)|ja|毛抜形太刀〈無銘/〉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenukigata Tachi (Dazaifu Tenman-gū)|1=Kenukigata Tachi (Dazaifu Tenman-gū)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q28690704}} : Housed at [[Dazaifu Tenman-gū Shrine]], designated as an Important Cultural Property. ; Gold-Inlaid Lacquer Kenukigata Tachi : Considered the finest example among surviving Kenukigata Tachi, housed at [[:en:Kasuga-taisha|Kasuga-taisha]] Shrine in [[Nara]]. Despite severe rust, the scabbard features intricate [[:en:Raden|raden]] and lacquerwork. Restoration efforts during the shrine's 60th ceremonial rebuilding (2015–2016) were featured in a 2016 NHK documentary.<ref>[http://www.nhk.or.jp/docudocu/program/92393/2393pp6/ "Kasuga-taisha: Reviving a Golden Sword—Secrets of a Heian-Era Treasure"] (NHK).</ref>
== Notes == * A Kenukigata Tachi attributed to [[:en:Taira no Tsunemori|Taira no Tsunemori]] is housed at [[:en:Chikubu Island|Chikubu Island]], though it is in poor condition. * A replica of a crimson Kenukigata Tachi can be viewed at the [[:en:Tokyo National Museum|Tokyo National Museum]]. * {{ill|Sasaki Tadakiyo|ja|佐々木忠慧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasaki Tadakiyo|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a professor at [[:en:Miyagi Gakuin Women's University|Miyagi Gakuin Women's University]], suggested in 1990 that the phrase "tachihatamatsukuri" in [[:en:The Pillow Book|The Pillow Book]] refers to swords crafted by the Moegusa smiths. This interpretation highlights the importance of Emishi craftsmanship in the development of Japanese swords.
== Footnotes ==
{{Reflist|group="Primary"}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * ''The Growth of Warriors and the Insei Period,'' Ryūhiko Shimomukai, 2001, [[:en:Kōdansha|Kōdansha]]. ISBN 4-06-268907-3. * ''-Special Exhibition- Birth of Musashi Bushi,'' Saitama Prefectural Museum of History and Folklore, 2009.

Initially, the warabite-tō were straight-bladed swords (chokutō), but their design evolved into curved blades better suited for mounted combat (jikku zangeki-sen) by striking downward while riding.

The transitional design, known as the "Kenukigata Warabite-tō," added an openwork pattern to the handle, improving grip and mitigating the shock of impact during strikes. This modification likely originated with the Emishi in the early 9th century. Subsequently, the decorative "warabi" motif on the handle disappeared, leading to the "Kenukigata-tō," which further emphasized functionality. The Kenukigata-tō is thought to have been developed by the Emishi by the late 9th century.
The {{ill|Moegusa|ja|舞草|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moegusa|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 一関市|1=Ichinoseki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q390817}} area (modern-day [[Ichinoseki City]]) became a hub for crafting these swords, and the smiths there were referred to as the Moegusa Forgers (舞草鍛冶).
The oldest example of the Kenukigata Tachi was unearthed in {{ill|Soga|en|Soga|WD=Q423436|lt=Soga}}, {{ill|Shiojiri, Nagano|en|Shiojiri, Nagano|WD=Q857272|lt=Shiojiri}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|en|Nagano Prefecture|WD=Q127877|lt=Nagano Prefecture}}, and is estimated to date back to the 10th century. It rapidly gained popularity and was adopted as a standard ceremonial weapon for {{ill|Royal guard|ja|衛府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Royal guard|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近衛兵|1=royal guard|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q971045}} (imperial guards). The emergence of this sword marked a transition from Ritsuryō-era tactics and contributed to the formation of Japan's medieval warrior class.
== Cultural Properties == ; {{ill|Kenukigata Tachi (Important Cultural Property No. 1354)|ja|毛抜形太刀 (重要文化財工第1354号)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenukigata Tachi (Important Cultural Property No. 1354)|1=Kenuki-gata tachi at Ise Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q28688501}} : Housed at [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Shrine]], designated as an Important Cultural Property. ; {{ill|Kenukigata Tachi (Hōgon-ji)|ja|毛抜形太刀〈無銘（伝Fujiwara no Hidesato奉納）/〉附梨子地桐紋蒔絵鞘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenukigata Tachi (Hōgon-ji)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} : Housed at [[Hōgon-ji Temple]], designated as an Important Cultural Property. ; {{ill|Kenukigata Tachi (Dazaifu Tenman-gū)|ja|毛抜形太刀〈無銘/〉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenukigata Tachi (Dazaifu Tenman-gū)|1=Kenukigata Tachi (Dazaifu Tenman-gū)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q28690704}} : Housed at [[Dazaifu Tenman-gū Shrine]], designated as an Important Cultural Property. ; Gold-Inlaid Lacquer Kenukigata Tachi : Considered the finest example among surviving Kenukigata Tachi, housed at {{ill|Kasuga-taisha|en|Kasuga-taisha|WD=Q714559|lt=Kasuga-taisha}} Shrine in [[Nara]]. Despite severe rust, the scabbard features intricate {{ill|Raden|en|Raden|WD=Q860235|lt=raden}} and lacquerwork. Restoration efforts during the shrine's 60th ceremonial rebuilding (2015–2016) were featured in a 2016 NHK documentary.<ref>[http://www.nhk.or.jp/docudocu/program/92393/2393pp6/ "Kasuga-taisha: Reviving a Golden Sword—Secrets of a Heian-Era Treasure"] (NHK).</ref>
== Notes == * A Kenukigata Tachi attributed to {{ill|Taira no Tsunemori|en|Taira no Tsunemori|WD=Q11061237|lt=Taira no Tsunemori}} is housed at {{ill|Chikubu Island|en|Chikubu Island|WD=Q2975401|lt=Chikubu Island}}, though it is in poor condition. * A replica of a crimson Kenukigata Tachi can be viewed at the {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|en|Tokyo National Museum|WD=Q653433|lt=Tokyo National Museum}}. * {{ill|Sasaki Tadakiyo|ja|佐々木忠慧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasaki Tadakiyo|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a professor at {{ill|Miyagi Gakuin Women's University|en|Miyagi Gakuin Women's University|WD=Q6884188|lt=Miyagi Gakuin Women's University}}, suggested in 1990 that the phrase "tachihatamatsukuri" in {{ill|The Pillow Book|en|The Pillow Book|WD=Q615137|lt=The Pillow Book}} refers to swords crafted by the Moegusa smiths. This interpretation highlights the importance of Emishi craftsmanship in the development of Japanese swords.
== Footnotes ==
{{Reflist|group="Primary"}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * ''The Growth of Warriors and the Insei Period,'' Ryūhiko Shimomukai, 2001, [[:en:Kōdansha|Kōdansha]]. ISBN 4-06-268907-3. * ''-Special Exhibition- Birth of Musashi Bushi,'' Saitama Prefectural Museum of History and Folklore, 2009.

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kenukigata tachi]][[ja:毛抜形太刀]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kenukigata tachi]][[ja:毛抜形太刀]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Kenukigata tachi]][[ja:毛抜形太刀]]
'''Kenukigata Tachi''' (毛抜形太刀) refers to a style of [[tachi]] (long sword) that emerged during the mid-{{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}. It is considered the prototype of the [[Japanese sword]] (nihonto). The sword is crafted as a single unit, with the handle (made of iron) and blade forged together (referred to as kyōtetsuzukuri).
The sword is also known as the "'''Efu no Tachi'''” (衛府の太刀). Some sources claim it is referred to as "'''Fushū no Ken'''” (俘囚野剣) in the {{ill|Shirakawa Jōkō Kōya Gokōki|ja|白河上皇高野御幸記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirakawa Jōkō Kōya Gokōki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}; however, no mention of this term is found in the original text housed at Kōyasan Seinōin, {{Reflist|group="Primary"}}.
== Formation == The direct origin of the Kenukigata Tachi is traced to the [[Kofun period]]'s {{ill|Warabite-tō|ja|蕨手刀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Warabite-tō|1=warabitetō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11621998}}, used by the {{ill|Emishi|en|Emishi|WD=Q932574|lt=Emishi}} of northeastern Japan. Initially, the warabite-tō were straight-bladed swords (chokutō), but their design evolved into curved blades better suited for mounted combat (jikku zangeki-sen) by striking downward while riding.
When the {{ill|Imperial Court|en|Imperial Court|WD=Q6006523|lt=Imperial Court}} subdued the northeastern region, the Emishi's weapons and tactics influenced the Japanese people. Over time, the warabite-tō underwent refinements that eventually culminated in the Kenukigata Tachi, widely regarded as the prototype of the Japanese sword.
The transitional design, known as the "Kenukigata Warabite-tō," added an openwork pattern to the handle, improving grip and mitigating the shock of impact during strikes. This modification likely originated with the Emishi in the early 9th century. Subsequently, the decorative "warabi" motif on the handle disappeared, leading to the "Kenukigata-tō," which further emphasized functionality. The Kenukigata-tō is thought to have been developed by the Emishi by the late 9th century.
The {{ill|Moegusa|ja|舞草|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moegusa|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 一関市|1=Ichinoseki|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q390817}} area (modern-day [[Ichinoseki City]]) became a hub for crafting these swords, and the smiths there were referred to as the Moegusa Forgers (舞草鍛冶).
The oldest example of the Kenukigata Tachi was unearthed in {{ill|Soga|en|Soga|WD=Q423436|lt=Soga}}, {{ill|Shiojiri, Nagano|en|Shiojiri, Nagano|WD=Q857272|lt=Shiojiri}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|en|Nagano Prefecture|WD=Q127877|lt=Nagano Prefecture}}, and is estimated to date back to the 10th century. Archaeological finds and preserved examples are limited to areas west of the Kantō region.
=== Emergence === The Kenukigata Tachi evolved into a longer sword during the late 9th to early 10th century, coinciding with regional conflicts in the East. It rapidly gained popularity and was adopted as a standard ceremonial weapon for {{ill|Royal guard|ja|衛府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Royal guard|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近衛兵|1=royal guard|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q971045}} (imperial guards). The emergence of this sword marked a transition from Ritsuryō-era tactics and contributed to the formation of Japan's medieval warrior class.
== Cultural Properties == ; {{ill|Kenukigata Tachi (Important Cultural Property No. 1354)|ja|毛抜形太刀 (重要文化財工第1354号)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenukigata Tachi (Important Cultural Property No. 1354)|1=Kenuki-gata tachi at Ise Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q28688501}} : Housed at [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Shrine]], designated as an Important Cultural Property. ; {{ill|Kenukigata Tachi (Hōgon-ji)|ja|毛抜形太刀〈無銘（伝Fujiwara no Hidesato奉納）/〉附梨子地桐紋蒔絵鞘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenukigata Tachi (Hōgon-ji)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} : Housed at [[Hōgon-ji Temple]], designated as an Important Cultural Property. ; {{ill|Kenukigata Tachi (Dazaifu Tenman-gū)|ja|毛抜形太刀〈無銘/〉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenukigata Tachi (Dazaifu Tenman-gū)|1=Kenukigata Tachi (Dazaifu Tenman-gū)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q28690704}} : Housed at [[Dazaifu Tenman-gū Shrine]], designated as an Important Cultural Property. ; Gold-Inlaid Lacquer Kenukigata Tachi : Considered the finest example among surviving Kenukigata Tachi, housed at {{ill|Kasuga-taisha|en|Kasuga-taisha|WD=Q714559|lt=Kasuga-taisha}} Shrine in [[Nara]]. Despite severe rust, the scabbard features intricate {{ill|Raden|en|Raden|WD=Q860235|lt=raden}} and lacquerwork. Restoration efforts during the shrine's 60th ceremonial rebuilding (2015–2016) were featured in a 2016 NHK documentary.<ref>[http://www.nhk.or.jp/docudocu/program/92393/2393pp6/ "Kasuga-taisha: Reviving a Golden Sword—Secrets of a Heian-Era Treasure"] (NHK).</ref>
== Notes == * A Kenukigata Tachi attributed to {{ill|Taira no Tsunemori|en|Taira no Tsunemori|WD=Q11061237|lt=Taira no Tsunemori}} is housed at {{ill|Chikubu Island|en|Chikubu Island|WD=Q2975401|lt=Chikubu Island}}, though it is in poor condition. * A replica of a crimson Kenukigata Tachi can be viewed at the {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|en|Tokyo National Museum|WD=Q653433|lt=Tokyo National Museum}}. * {{ill|Sasaki Tadakiyo|ja|佐々木忠慧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasaki Tadakiyo|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a professor at {{ill|Miyagi Gakuin Women's University|en|Miyagi Gakuin Women's University|WD=Q6884188|lt=Miyagi Gakuin Women's University}}, suggested in 1990 that the phrase "tachihatamatsukuri" in {{ill|The Pillow Book|en|The Pillow Book|WD=Q615137|lt=The Pillow Book}} refers to swords crafted by the Moegusa smiths. This interpretation highlights the importance of Emishi craftsmanship in the development of Japanese swords.
== Footnotes ==
{{Reflist|group="Primary"}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * ''The Growth of Warriors and the Insei Period,'' Ryūhiko Shimomukai, 2001, [[:en:Kōdansha|Kōdansha]]. ISBN 4-06-268907-3. * ''-Special Exhibition- Birth of Musashi Bushi,'' Saitama Prefectural Museum of History and Folklore, 2009.

Despite severe rust, the scabbard features intricate {{ill|Raden|en|Raden|WD=Q860235|lt=raden}} and lacquerwork. Restoration efforts during the shrine's 60th ceremonial rebuilding (2015–2016) were featured in a 2016 NHK documentary.<ref>[http://www.nhk.or.jp/docudocu/program/92393/2393pp6/ "Kasuga-taisha: Reviving a Golden Sword—Secrets of a Heian-Era Treasure"] (NHK).</ref>
== Notes == * A Kenukigata Tachi attributed to {{ill|Taira no Tsunemori|en|Taira no Tsunemori|WD=Q11061237|lt=Taira no Tsunemori}} is housed at {{ill|Chikubu Island|en|Chikubu Island|WD=Q2975401|lt=Chikubu Island}}, though it is in poor condition. * A replica of a crimson Kenukigata Tachi can be viewed at the {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|en|Tokyo National Museum|WD=Q653433|lt=Tokyo National Museum}}. * {{ill|Sasaki Tadakiyo|ja|佐々木忠慧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasaki Tadakiyo|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a professor at {{ill|Miyagi Gakuin Women's University|en|Miyagi Gakuin Women's University|WD=Q6884188|lt=Miyagi Gakuin Women's University}}, suggested in 1990 that the phrase "tachihatamatsukuri" in {{ill|The Pillow Book|en|The Pillow Book|WD=Q615137|lt=The Pillow Book}} refers to swords crafted by the Moegusa smiths. This interpretation highlights the importance of Emishi craftsmanship in the development of Japanese swords.
== Footnotes ==
{{Reflist|group="Primary"}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * ''The Growth of Warriors and the Insei Period,'' Ryūhiko Shimomukai, 2001, [[:en:Kōdansha|Kōdansha]]. ISBN 4-06-268907-3. * ''-Special Exhibition- Birth of Musashi Bushi,'' Saitama Prefectural Museum of History and Folklore, 2009.

revision.comment: Bot: fix ill template destination links
revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kenukigata tachi]][[ja:毛抜形太刀]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kenukigata tachi]][[ja:毛抜形太刀]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[sn:commons:Category:Kenukigata tachi]][[ja:毛抜形太刀]]
'''Kenukigata Tachi''' (毛抜形太刀) refers to a style of [[tachi]] (long sword) that emerged during the mid-{{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}. It is considered the prototype of the [[Japanese sword]] (nihonto). The sword is crafted as a single unit, with the handle (made of iron) and blade forged together (referred to as kyōtetsuzukuri).
The sword is also known as the "'''Efu no Tachi'''” (衛府の太刀). Some sources claim it is referred to as "'''Fushū no Ken'''” (俘囚野剣) in the {{ill|Shirakawa Jōkō Kōya Gokōki|ja|白河上皇高野御幸記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirakawa Jōkō Kōya Gokōki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}; however, no mention of this term is found in the original text housed at Kōyasan Seinōin, {{Reflist|group="Primary"}}.
== Formation == The direct origin of the Kenukigata Tachi is traced to the [[Kofun period]]'s {{ill|Warabite-tō|ja|蕨手刀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Warabite-tō|1=Warabite Katana|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11621998}}, used by the {{ill|Emishi|en|Emishi|WD=Q932574|lt=Emishi|1=Emishi}} of northeastern Japan. Initially, the warabite-tō were straight-bladed swords (chokutō), but their design evolved into curved blades better suited for mounted combat (jikku zangeki-sen) by striking downward while riding.
When the {{ill|Imperial Court|en|Imperial Court|WD=Q6006523|lt=Imperial Court|1=Imperial Court}} subdued the northeastern region, the Emishi's weapons and tactics influenced the Japanese people. Over time, the warabite-tō underwent refinements that eventually culminated in the Kenukigata Tachi, widely regarded as the prototype of the Japanese sword.
The transitional design, known as the "Kenukigata Warabite-tō," added an openwork pattern to the handle, improving grip and mitigating the shock of impact during strikes. This modification likely originated with the Emishi in the early 9th century. Subsequently, the decorative "warabi" motif on the handle disappeared, leading to the "Kenukigata-tō," which further emphasized functionality. The Kenukigata-tō is thought to have been developed by the Emishi by the late 9th century.
The {{ill|Moegusa|ja|舞草|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moegusa|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 一関市|1=Ichinoseki, Iwate|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q390817}} area (modern-day [[Ichinoseki City]]) became a hub for crafting these swords, and the smiths there were referred to as the Moegusa Forgers (舞草鍛冶).
The oldest example of the Kenukigata Tachi was unearthed in {{ill|Soga|en|Soga|WD=Q423436|lt=Soga|1=Soga}}, {{ill|Shiojiri, Nagano|en|Shiojiri, Nagano|WD=Q857272|lt=Shiojiri|1=Shiojiri, Nagano}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|en|Nagano Prefecture|WD=Q127877|lt=Nagano Prefecture|1=Nagano Prefecture}}, and is estimated to date back to the 10th century. Archaeological finds and preserved examples are limited to areas west of the Kantō region.
=== Emergence === The Kenukigata Tachi evolved into a longer sword during the late 9th to early 10th century, coinciding with regional conflicts in the East. It rapidly gained popularity and was adopted as a standard ceremonial weapon for {{ill|Royal guard|ja|衛府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Royal guard|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近衛兵|1=Royal guard|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q971045}} (imperial guards). The emergence of this sword marked a transition from Ritsuryō-era tactics and contributed to the formation of Japan's medieval warrior class.
== Cultural Properties == ; {{ill|Kenukigata Tachi (Important Cultural Property No. 1354)|ja|毛抜形太刀 (重要文化財工第1354号)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenukigata Tachi (Important Cultural Property No. 1354)|1=Kenuki-gata tachi at Ise Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q28688501}} : Housed at [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Shrine]], designated as an Important Cultural Property. ; {{ill|Kenukigata Tachi (Hōgon-ji)|ja|毛抜形太刀〈無銘（伝Fujiwara no Hidesato奉納）/〉附梨子地桐紋蒔絵鞘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenukigata Tachi (Hōgon-ji)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} : Housed at [[Hōgon-ji Temple]], designated as an Important Cultural Property. ; {{ill|Kenukigata Tachi (Dazaifu Tenman-gū)|ja|毛抜形太刀〈無銘/〉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenukigata Tachi (Dazaifu Tenman-gū)|1=Q28690704|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q28690704}} : Housed at [[Dazaifu Tenman-gū Shrine]], designated as an Important Cultural Property. ; Gold-Inlaid Lacquer Kenukigata Tachi : Considered the finest example among surviving Kenukigata Tachi, housed at {{ill|Kasuga-taisha|en|Kasuga-taisha|WD=Q714559|lt=Kasuga-taisha|1=Kasuga-taisha}} Shrine in [[Nara]]. Despite severe rust, the scabbard features intricate {{ill|Raden|en|Raden|WD=Q860235|lt=raden|1=Raden}} and lacquerwork. Restoration efforts during the shrine's 60th ceremonial rebuilding (2015–2016) were featured in a 2016 NHK documentary.<ref>[http://www.nhk.or.jp/docudocu/program/92393/2393pp6/ "Kasuga-taisha: Reviving a Golden Sword—Secrets of a Heian-Era Treasure"] (NHK).</ref>
== Notes == * A Kenukigata Tachi attributed to {{ill|Taira no Tsunemori|en|Taira no Tsunemori|WD=Q11061237|lt=Taira no Tsunemori|1=Taira no Tsunemori}} is housed at {{ill|Chikubu Island|en|Chikubu Island|WD=Q2975401|lt=Chikubu Island|1=Chikubu Island}}, though it is in poor condition. * A replica of a crimson Kenukigata Tachi can be viewed at the {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|en|Tokyo National Museum|WD=Q653433|lt=Tokyo National Museum|1=Tokyo National Museum}}. * {{ill|Sasaki Tadakiyo|ja|佐々木忠慧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasaki Tadakiyo|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a professor at {{ill|Miyagi Gakuin Women's University|en|Miyagi Gakuin Women's University|WD=Q6884188|lt=Miyagi Gakuin Women's University|1=Miyagi Gakuin Women's University}}, suggested in 1990 that the phrase "tachihatamatsukuri" in {{ill|The Pillow Book|en|The Pillow Book|WD=Q615137|lt=The Pillow Book|1=The Pillow Book}} refers to swords crafted by the Moegusa smiths. This interpretation highlights the importance of Emishi craftsmanship in the development of Japanese swords.
== Footnotes ==
{{Reflist|group="Primary"}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * ''The Growth of Warriors and the Insei Period,'' Ryūhiko Shimomukai, 2001, [[:en:Kōdansha|Kōdansha]]. ISBN 4-06-268907-3. * ''-Special Exhibition- Birth of Musashi Bushi,'' Saitama Prefectural Museum of History and Folklore, 2009.

<!--Mounuki-type swords--><!--Mounuki-type swords--><!--Mounuki-type swords--><!--Mounuki-type swords--><!--Mounuki-type swords--><!--Mounuki-type swords--> <!--jawiki derived wikidata interwikis--> {{wikidata link|Q11547735}} [[Category:Completion tier 2]] [[Category:Japanese swords]] [[Category:Wikidata no short description]] [[Category:pages]]

revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kenukigata tachi]][[ja:毛抜形太刀]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Kenukigata tachi]][[ja:毛抜形太刀]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[Category:sn_crud|TESTcommons:Category:Kenukigata tachi]][[ja:毛抜形太刀]]
'''Kenukigata Tachi''' (毛抜形太刀) refers to a style of [[tachi]] (long sword) that emerged during the mid-{{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}. It is considered the prototype of the [[Japanese sword]] (nihonto). The sword is crafted as a single unit, with the handle (made of iron) and blade forged together (referred to as kyōtetsuzukuri).
The sword is also known as the "'''Efu no Tachi'''” (衛府の太刀). Some sources claim it is referred to as "'''Fushū no Ken'''” (俘囚野剣) in the {{ill|Shirakawa Jōkō Kōya Gokōki|ja|白河上皇高野御幸記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirakawa Jōkō Kōya Gokōki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}; however, no mention of this term is found in the original text housed at Kōyasan Seinōin, {{Reflist|group="Primary"}}.
== Formation == The direct origin of the Kenukigata Tachi is traced to the [[Kofun period]]'s {{ill|Warabite-tō|ja|蕨手刀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Warabite-tō|1=Warabite Katana|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11621998}}, used by the {{ill|Emishi|en|Emishi|WD=Q932574|lt=Emishi|1=Emishi}} of northeastern Japan. Initially, the warabite-tō were straight-bladed swords (chokutō), but their design evolved into curved blades better suited for mounted combat (jikku zangeki-sen) by striking downward while riding.
When the {{ill|Imperial Court|en|Imperial Court|WD=Q6006523|lt=Imperial Court|1=Imperial Court}} subdued the northeastern region, the Emishi's weapons and tactics influenced the Japanese people. Over time, the warabite-tō underwent refinements that eventually culminated in the Kenukigata Tachi, widely regarded as the prototype of the Japanese sword.
The transitional design, known as the "Kenukigata Warabite-tō," added an openwork pattern to the handle, improving grip and mitigating the shock of impact during strikes. This modification likely originated with the Emishi in the early 9th century. Subsequently, the decorative "warabi" motif on the handle disappeared, leading to the "Kenukigata-tō," which further emphasized functionality. The Kenukigata-tō is thought to have been developed by the Emishi by the late 9th century.
The {{ill|Moegusa|ja|舞草|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moegusa|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 一関市|1=Ichinoseki, Iwate|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q390817}} area (modern-day [[Ichinoseki City]]) became a hub for crafting these swords, and the smiths there were referred to as the Moegusa Forgers (舞草鍛冶).
The oldest example of the Kenukigata Tachi was unearthed in {{ill|Soga|en|Soga|WD=Q423436|lt=Soga|1=Soga}}, {{ill|Shiojiri, Nagano|en|Shiojiri, Nagano|WD=Q857272|lt=Shiojiri|1=Shiojiri, Nagano}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|en|Nagano Prefecture|WD=Q127877|lt=Nagano Prefecture|1=Nagano Prefecture}}, and is estimated to date back to the 10th century. Archaeological finds and preserved examples are limited to areas west of the Kantō region.
=== Emergence === The Kenukigata Tachi evolved into a longer sword during the late 9th to early 10th century, coinciding with regional conflicts in the East. It rapidly gained popularity and was adopted as a standard ceremonial weapon for {{ill|Royal guard|ja|衛府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Royal guard|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近衛兵|1=Royal guard|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q971045}} (imperial guards). The emergence of this sword marked a transition from Ritsuryō-era tactics and contributed to the formation of Japan's medieval warrior class.
== Cultural Properties == ; {{ill|Kenukigata Tachi (Important Cultural Property No. 1354)|ja|毛抜形太刀 (重要文化財工第1354号)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenukigata Tachi (Important Cultural Property No. 1354)|1=Kenuki-gata tachi at Ise Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q28688501}} : Housed at [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Shrine]], designated as an Important Cultural Property. ; {{ill|Kenukigata Tachi (Hōgon-ji)|ja|毛抜形太刀〈無銘（伝Fujiwara no Hidesato奉納）/〉附梨子地桐紋蒔絵鞘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenukigata Tachi (Hōgon-ji)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} : Housed at [[Hōgon-ji Temple]], designated as an Important Cultural Property. ; {{ill|Kenukigata Tachi (Dazaifu Tenman-gū)|ja|毛抜形太刀〈無銘/〉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenukigata Tachi (Dazaifu Tenman-gū)|1=Q28690704|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q28690704}} : Housed at [[Dazaifu Tenman-gū Shrine]], designated as an Important Cultural Property. ; Gold-Inlaid Lacquer Kenukigata Tachi : Considered the finest example among surviving Kenukigata Tachi, housed at {{ill|Kasuga-taisha|en|Kasuga-taisha|WD=Q714559|lt=Kasuga-taisha|1=Kasuga-taisha}} Shrine in [[Nara]]. Despite severe rust, the scabbard features intricate {{ill|Raden|en|Raden|WD=Q860235|lt=raden|1=Raden}} and lacquerwork. Restoration efforts during the shrine's 60th ceremonial rebuilding (2015–2016) were featured in a 2016 NHK documentary.<ref>[http://www.nhk.or.jp/docudocu/program/92393/2393pp6/ "Kasuga-taisha: Reviving a Golden Sword—Secrets of a Heian-Era Treasure"] (NHK).</ref>
== Notes == * A Kenukigata Tachi attributed to {{ill|Taira no Tsunemori|en|Taira no Tsunemori|WD=Q11061237|lt=Taira no Tsunemori|1=Taira no Tsunemori}} is housed at {{ill|Chikubu Island|en|Chikubu Island|WD=Q2975401|lt=Chikubu Island|1=Chikubu Island}}, though it is in poor condition. * A replica of a crimson Kenukigata Tachi can be viewed at the {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|en|Tokyo National Museum|WD=Q653433|lt=Tokyo National Museum|1=Tokyo National Museum}}. * {{ill|Sasaki Tadakiyo|ja|佐々木忠慧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasaki Tadakiyo|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a professor at {{ill|Miyagi Gakuin Women's University|en|Miyagi Gakuin Women's University|WD=Q6884188|lt=Miyagi Gakuin Women's University|1=Miyagi Gakuin Women's University}}, suggested in 1990 that the phrase "tachihatamatsukuri" in {{ill|The Pillow Book|en|The Pillow Book|WD=Q615137|lt=The Pillow Book|1=The Pillow Book}} refers to swords crafted by the Moegusa smiths. This interpretation highlights the importance of Emishi craftsmanship in the development of Japanese swords.
== Footnotes ==
{{Reflist|group="Primary"}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * ''The Growth of Warriors and the Insei Period,'' Ryūhiko Shimomukai, 2001, [[:en:Kōdansha|Kōdansha]]. ISBN 4-06-268907-3. * ''-Special Exhibition- Birth of Musashi Bushi,'' Saitama Prefectural Museum of History and Folklore, 2009.
{{translated page|ja|毛抜形太刀|version=103649806|comment=Imported full ja history}}

The sword is also known as the "'''Efu no Tachi'''” (衛府の太刀). Some sources claim it is referred to as "'''Fushū no Ken'''” (俘囚野剣) in the {{ill|Shirakawa Jōkō Kōya Gokōki|ja|白河上皇高野御幸記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirakawa Jōkō Kōya Gokōki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}; however, no mention of this term is found in the original text housed at Kōyasan Seinōin, {{Reflist|group="Primary"}}.
== Formation == The direct origin of the Kenukigata Tachi is traced to the [[Kofun period]]'s {{ill|Warabite-tō|ja|蕨手刀|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Warabite-tō|1=Warabite Katana|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11621998}}, used by the {{ill|Emishi|en|Emishi|qid=Q932574|lt=Emishi|1=Emishi}} of northeastern Japan. Initially, the warabite-tō were straight-bladed swords (chokutō), but their design evolved into curved blades better suited for mounted combat (jikku zangeki-sen) by striking downward while riding.
When the {{ill|Imperial Court|en|Imperial Court|qid=Q6006523|lt=Imperial Court|1=Imperial Court}} subdued the northeastern region, the Emishi's weapons and tactics influenced the Japanese people. Over time, the warabite-tō underwent refinements that eventually culminated in the Kenukigata Tachi, widely regarded as the prototype of the Japanese sword.
The transitional design, known as the "Kenukigata Warabite-tō," added an openwork pattern to the handle, improving grip and mitigating the shock of impact during strikes. This modification likely originated with the Emishi in the early 9th century. Subsequently, the decorative "warabi" motif on the handle disappeared, leading to the "Kenukigata-tō," which further emphasized functionality. The Kenukigata-tō is thought to have been developed by the Emishi by the late 9th century.
The {{ill|Moegusa|ja|舞草|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moegusa|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 一関市|1=Ichinoseki, Iwate|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q390817}} area (modern-day [[Ichinoseki City]]) became a hub for crafting these swords, and the smiths there were referred to as the Moegusa Forgers (舞草鍛冶).
The oldest example of the Kenukigata Tachi was unearthed in {{ill|Soga|en|Soga|qid=Q423436|lt=Soga|1=Soga}}, {{ill|Shiojiri, Nagano|en|Shiojiri, Nagano|qid=Q857272|lt=Shiojiri|1=Shiojiri, Nagano}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|en|Nagano Prefecture|qid=Q127877|lt=Nagano Prefecture|1=Nagano Prefecture}}, and is estimated to date back to the 10th century. Archaeological finds and preserved examples are limited to areas west of the Kantō region.
=== Emergence === The Kenukigata Tachi evolved into a longer sword during the late 9th to early 10th century, coinciding with regional conflicts in the East. It rapidly gained popularity and was adopted as a standard ceremonial weapon for {{ill|Royal guard|ja|衛府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Royal guard|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近衛兵|1=Royal guard|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q971045}} (imperial guards). The emergence of this sword marked a transition from Ritsuryō-era tactics and contributed to the formation of Japan's medieval warrior class.
== Cultural Properties == ; {{ill|Kenukigata Tachi (Important Cultural Property No. 1354)|ja|毛抜形太刀 (重要文化財工第1354号)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenukigata Tachi (Important Cultural Property No. 1354)|1=Kenuki-gata tachi at Ise Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q28688501}} : Housed at [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Shrine]], designated as an Important Cultural Property. ; {{ill|Kenukigata Tachi (Hōgon-ji)|ja|毛抜形太刀〈無銘（伝Fujiwara no Hidesato奉納）/〉附梨子地桐紋蒔絵鞘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenukigata Tachi (Hōgon-ji)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} : Housed at [[Hōgon-ji Temple]], designated as an Important Cultural Property. ; {{ill|Kenukigata Tachi (Dazaifu Tenman-gū)|ja|毛抜形太刀〈無銘/〉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenukigata Tachi (Dazaifu Tenman-gū)|1=Q28690704|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q28690704}} : Housed at [[Dazaifu Tenman-gū Shrine]], designated as an Important Cultural Property. ; Gold-Inlaid Lacquer Kenukigata Tachi : Considered the finest example among surviving Kenukigata Tachi, housed at {{ill|Kasuga-taisha|en|Kasuga-taisha|qid=Q714559|lt=Kasuga-taisha|1=Kasuga-taisha}} Shrine in [[Nara]]. Despite severe rust, the scabbard features intricate {{ill|Raden|en|Raden|qid=Q860235|lt=raden|1=Raden}} and lacquerwork. Restoration efforts during the shrine's 60th ceremonial rebuilding (2015–2016) were featured in a 2016 NHK documentary.<ref>[http://www.nhk.or.jp/docudocu/program/92393/2393pp6/ "Kasuga-taisha: Reviving a Golden Sword—Secrets of a Heian-Era Treasure"] (NHK).</ref>
== Notes == * A Kenukigata Tachi attributed to {{ill|Taira no Tsunemori|en|Taira no Tsunemori|qid=Q11061237|lt=Taira no Tsunemori|1=Taira no Tsunemori}} is housed at {{ill|Chikubu Island|en|Chikubu Island|qid=Q2975401|lt=Chikubu Island|1=Chikubu Island}}, though it is in poor condition. * A replica of a crimson Kenukigata Tachi can be viewed at the {{ill|Tokyo National Museum|en|Tokyo National Museum|qid=Q653433|lt=Tokyo National Museum|1=Tokyo National Museum}}. * {{ill|Sasaki Tadakiyo|ja|佐々木忠慧|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sasaki Tadakiyo|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}, a professor at {{ill|Miyagi Gakuin Women's University|en|Miyagi Gakuin Women's University|qid=Q6884188|lt=Miyagi Gakuin Women's University|1=Miyagi Gakuin Women's University}}, suggested in 1990 that the phrase "tachihatamatsukuri" in {{ill|The Pillow Book|en|The Pillow Book|qid=Q615137|lt=The Pillow Book|1=The Pillow Book}} refers to swords crafted by the Moegusa smiths. This interpretation highlights the importance of Emishi craftsmanship in the development of Japanese swords.
== Footnotes ==
{{Reflist|group="Primary"}} {{Reflist}}
== References == * ''The Growth of Warriors and the Insei Period,'' Ryūhiko Shimomukai, 2001, [[:en:Kōdansha|Kōdansha]]. ISBN 4-06-268907-3. * ''-Special Exhibition- Birth of Musashi Bushi,'' Saitama Prefectural Museum of History and Folklore, 2009.

revision.text:
'''Kenukigata Tachi''' (毛抜形太刀) refers to a style of [[tachi]] (long sword) that emerged during the mid-{{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|qid=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}. It is considered the prototype of the [[Japanese sword]] (nihonto). The sword is crafted as a single unit, with the handle (made of iron) and blade forged together (referred to as kyōtetsuzukuri).
The sword is also known as the "'''Efu no Tachi'''” (衛府の太刀). Some sources claim it is referred to as "'''Fushū no Ken'''” (俘囚野剣) in the {{ill|Shirakawa Jōkō Kōya Gokōki|ja|白河上皇高野御幸記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shirakawa Jōkō Kōya Gokōki|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}}; however, no mention of this term is found in the original text housed at Kōyasan Seinōin, {{Reflist|group="Primary"}}.

Initially, the warabite-tō were straight-bladed swords (chokutō), but their design evolved into curved blades better suited for mounted combat (jikku zangeki-sen) by striking downward while riding.
When the {{ill|Imperial Court|en|Imperial Court|qid=Q6006523|lt=Imperial Court|1=Imperial Court}} subdued the northeastern region, the Emishi's weapons and tactics influenced the Japanese people. Over time, the warabite-tō underwent refinements that eventually culminated in the Kenukigata Tachi, widely regarded as the prototype of the Japanese sword.
The transitional design, known as the "Kenukigata Warabite-tō," added an openwork pattern to the handle, improving grip and mitigating the shock of impact during strikes. This modification likely originated with the Emishi in the early 9th century. Subsequently, the decorative "warabi" motif on the handle disappeared, leading to the "Kenukigata-tō," which further emphasized functionality. The Kenukigata-tō is thought to have been developed by the Emishi by the late 9th century.
The {{ill|Moegusa|ja|舞草|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Moegusa|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 一関市|1=Ichinoseki, Iwate|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q390817}} area (modern-day [[Ichinoseki City]]) became a hub for crafting these swords, and the smiths there were referred to as the Moegusa Forgers (舞草鍛冶).
The oldest example of the Kenukigata Tachi was unearthed in {{ill|Soga|en|Soga|qid=Q423436|lt=Soga|1=Soga}}, {{ill|Shiojiri, Nagano|en|Shiojiri, Nagano|qid=Q857272|lt=Shiojiri|1=Shiojiri, Nagano}}, {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|en|Nagano Prefecture|qid=Q127877|lt=Nagano Prefecture|1=Nagano Prefecture}}, and is estimated to date back to the 10th century. Archaeological finds and preserved examples are limited to areas west of the Kantō region.
=== Emergence === The Kenukigata Tachi evolved into a longer sword during the late 9th to early 10th century, coinciding with regional conflicts in the East. It rapidly gained popularity and was adopted as a standard ceremonial weapon for {{ill|Royal guard|ja|衛府|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Royal guard|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 近衛兵|1=Royal guard|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q971045}} (imperial guards). The emergence of this sword marked a transition from Ritsuryō-era tactics and contributed to the formation of Japan's medieval warrior class.
== Cultural Properties == ; {{ill|Kenukigata Tachi (Important Cultural Property No. 1354)|ja|毛抜形太刀 (重要文化財工第1354号)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenukigata Tachi (Important Cultural Property No. 1354)|1=Kenuki-gata tachi at Ise Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q28688501}} : Housed at [[Ise Grand Shrine|Ise Shrine]], designated as an Important Cultural Property. ; {{ill|Kenukigata Tachi (Hōgon-ji)|ja|毛抜形太刀〈無銘（伝Fujiwara no Hidesato奉納）/〉附梨子地桐紋蒔絵鞘|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenukigata Tachi (Hōgon-ji)|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} : Housed at [[Hōgon-ji Temple]], designated as an Important Cultural Property. ; {{ill|Kenukigata Tachi (Dazaifu Tenman-gū)|ja|毛抜形太刀〈無銘/〉|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kenukigata Tachi (Dazaifu Tenman-gū)|1=Q28690704|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q28690704}} : Housed at [[Dazaifu Tenman-gū Shrine]], designated as an Important Cultural Property. ; Gold-Inlaid Lacquer Kenukigata Tachi : Considered the finest example among surviving Kenukigata Tachi, housed at {{ill|Kasuga-taisha|en|Kasuga-taisha|qid=Q714559|lt=Kasuga-taisha|1=Kasuga-taisha}} Shrine in [[Nara]]. Despite severe rust, the scabbard features intricate {{ill|Raden|en|Raden|qid=Q860235|lt=raden|1=Raden}} and lacquerwork. Restoration efforts during the shrine's 60th ceremonial rebuilding (2015–2016) were featured in a 2016 NHK documentary.<ref>[http://www.nhk.or.jp/docudocu/program/92393/2393pp6/ "Kasuga-taisha: Reviving a Golden Sword—Secrets of a Heian-Era Treasure"] (NHK).</ref>

This interpretation highlights the importance of Emishi craftsmanship in the development of Japanese swords.
